segments.s654-g17: که begins a relative clause 'who'. The construction او را ... بود is an older Persian possessive/existential pattern literally 'there was to him ...' and so means 'he had ...'. یکی is a numeral/adjective 'one, a single' modifying the following noun. پاک is an adjective placed before دستور, giving 'pure instruction/code/rite'. بود is the past copula marking the clause in the past ('there was' → 'he had').
segments.s654-g18: که again introduces a descriptive clause ('who ...'). بیدار precedes the noun دل as an attributive adjective meaning 'alert/awake', so بیدار دل = 'of alert heart'. بود is the past copula ('was'). The coordinating و links the two predicates; گنجور is an adjectival/predicative word 'treasure‑laden / rich in treasure', followed by بود again to repeat the past copula.
segments.s654-g19: دلی here names the possessed faculty ('a heart'), with پرخرد (an adjectival compound 'full of wisdom/very wise') modifying it: دلی پرخرد داشت = 'he had a wise heart'. داشت is the past tense of 'to have'. The coordinating و links the next noun phrase رای درست, where رای ('counsel/opinion') is qualified by درست ('right/proper'), giving 'sound counsel'.
segments.s654-g20: ز گیتی is an ablative/ablative‑like phrase 'from the world' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). به جز functions with جز to mean 'except' (literally 'to/for except'), so ز گیتی به جز = 'from the world except'. نیکنامی is the object 'a good name / reputation'. نجست is the past of جستن 'to seek', used here in the sense 'he sought' (archaic literary form).
segments.s654-g21: که begins the relative/descriptive clause 'who...'. مهبود is an epithet literally 'moon‑like one' (a poetic epithet). بدنام follows as a postpositive adjective 'ill‑famed / notorious'. آن is the demonstrative/article 'that/the' pointing to the person; پاک modifies مغز (here 'پاک مغز' = 'pure‑minded' or 'pure of intellect'). Word order is characteristic of classical Persian epithets and adjectives stacked around the noun.
segments.s654-g22: روان and دلش are coordinated subjects 'his soul and his heart' (ش is the possessive enclitic 'his'). پر ز is an idiomatic poetic expression 'full of' (پر = 'full', followed by ز = 'of/from'). گفتار نغز is the noun phrase 'speech/utterance' qualified by نغز 'pithy/witty/delightful'. Together: 'his soul and heart were full of delightful speech.'
segments.s654-g23: دو فرزند بودش uses the older possessive/existential pattern بودش ('there were to him' / 'he had') to state 'he had two children'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', so چو خرم بهار = 'like the joyful spring'. The participial/poetic placement of the simile after بودش is typical of Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s654-g24: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. پرستنده is an active participle meaning 'worshipper/adoring one' or 'devoted follower' and here describes the children as constantly devoted; شهریار is the object/target 'the king'. Word order: adverb + participle + object (the participle functions adjectivally).
segments.s654-g33: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones' (elders/nobles). ز is the poetic preposition 'از' meaning 'of/from' that marks the target of their feeling (they were envious of Mahbud). بردند رشک is a compound-like expression: بردند (past plural 'they took/they had') + رشک (envy) — idiomatically 'they felt/envied'. The past verb بردند agrees with the plural subject بزرگان.
segments.s654-g34: همی‌ریختندی is an archaic/epic imperfective plural form (همی‑ prefix for continuous/habitual action + ریختندی) meaning 'they were pouring/throwing' or 'kept shedding'. The following prepositional phrase به رخ بر سرشک places the action onto faces: به رخ 'toward/on the face' (جهت), بر 'on/upon', سرشک 'teardrop(s)'. Word order is verb then directional phrases; subject is still بزرگان, so 'they poured teardrops on their faces.'
segments.s654-g35: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing someone). نامور بود is the predicate 'was renowned' (نامور adjective + بود past). زروان is the proper name Zurvān; به نام literally 'by the name' — here به functions like 'by/with the name of', so the phrase means 'a renowned one by the name of Zurvān.'
segments.s654-g36: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. او را uses the object marker را with the third‑person pronoun, marking the person affected in the clause. بدی is a noun 'evil/ill will' and here denotes ill disposition; بر در شاه کام is a prepositional phrase locating that ill will 'toward/against (بر) the gate/court (در) of the king (شاه) — i.e. the king's court or favor (کام = desire/approval)'. Altogether the clause means 'who bore ill will toward the king's court.'
segments.s654-g37: کهن بود is a simple adjectival predicate 'he was old' (کهن + بود past). و هم connects a second coordinated clause: هم 'also' precedes حاجب شاه to mark 'also chamberlain of the king'. The second بود again is the past copula agreeing with the same subject. Word order: adjective/role + بود, coordinated by و هم.
segments.s654-g38: فروزنده is an adjectival/agentive form 'radiant/illuminating' used as a title; رسم درگاه is a genitive construction ('the rites/customs of the court') where the ezāfe is omitted in older style. بود closes the clause as the past copula: 'he was the radiant one of the courtly rites.' The order is epithet + noun phrase + copula, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s654-g39: ز is the poetic form of از 'from/of' marking origin or genealogy: 'from Mahbud and Farrokh'. The names مهبود and فرخ are listed together as the two sons; دو فرزند is 'two children/sons'. اوی is the possessive 'his' (classical form placed after the noun), so the phrase reads 'from Mahbud and Farrokh, his two sons' i.e. 'his two sons, Mahbud and Farrokh.' Word order reflects older Persian where the genitive/possession elements can follow.
segments.s654-g40: همه‌ساله is an adverb 'every year/annually'. بودی is an archaic past-tense form agreeing with the (plural) subject 'the two sons' — in epic Persian such variant endings appear instead of the modern بودند. پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' and is followed by آبروی 'honor/reputation'. So the clause means 'each year they were full of honor.'
segments.s654-g161: ازان تازی اسبان is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb: ازان (from those) + تازی اسبان (Arabian horses). دلش is the subject/experiencer (دل + enclitic ش = 'his heart'), and برفروخت is a past, poetic/periphrastic verb meaning 'was kindled/was set on fire' (here intransitive, describing an emotional state). The verb comes after the noun phrase, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s654-g162: به مهبود is an indirect-object/benefactive phrase 'for/to the moon‑like one' (مهبود is a poetic epithet, 'moon‑like beloved'). بر جای مهرش is a locative/figurative phrase 'upon the place/seat of his love' (بر + جای + مهر + enclitic ش). بسوخت is the simple past intransitive 'burned/was consumed'; together the clauses say his love burned in the place reserved for that beloved. Note the order: beneficiary/target + locative + verb.
segments.s654-g163: فروریخت is a past tense verb 'fell/poured down' (here used of tears). آب از دو دیده is the theme/agent phrase 'water/tears from both eyes.' بدرد here functions adverbially/causally 'because of pain' (بـ + درد = 'in pain/owing to pain'), explaining the reason for the tears. Word order places the verb first (poetic) then the source and circumstance.
segments.s654-g164: بسی is an intensifier 'greatly/much' (classical adverb). داغ دل is a compound noun 'wound/burn of the heart' meaning deep grief. یاد مهبود is the object phrase 'the remembrance/memory of the moon‑like beloved' (یاد + مهبود), placed before the verb کرد ('made/caused' or 'did'), so that یاد مهبود کرد = 'caused/produced the heart's great grief' — کرد here is a light verb forming the predicate.
segments.s654-g165: چنین گفت is 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — here used narratively). کان introduces the clause 'that' (classical/connective), followed by مرد با جاه و رای 'the man with rank and counsel' (مرد = man/hero; با + جاه و رای = instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with rank and counsel'). The phrase is a nominative/topic placed before the following verb in the next segment.
segments.s654-g166: ببردش is the verbal phrase meaning 'he took/took away him/it' (verb + object enclitic ش). In classical narrative it can be read as past or potential depending on context; here rendered as 'would/so took him.' چنان 'thus/so' is adverbial, then دیو ریمن 'the demon Rīman' is the agent and ز جای 'from his place' is an ablative phrase showing removal. Word order: verb + object enclitic + adverbial details follow in a poetic, somewhat inverted sequence.
segments.s654-g167: بدان is the imperative 'know' formed from the prefix بـ + دان (classical compound for 'know this'). دوستداری here is used as a nominal idea 'that love/affection' (though it can be the 2nd-person verb 'you love'); in context it is a noun phrase paired with آن راستی 'and that truth' (آن = 'that', راستی = 'truth/rightness'). The phrase is an instruction/statement: 'Know (about) that love and that truth.'
segments.s654-g168: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. زد is the past tense of زدن 'to strike/affect' (here 'struck/affected'). روانش is 'his soul/spirit' (روان + enclitic ش). درکاستی is an adverbial/classical compound meaning 'from lack/for want' (در + کاستی = 'in deficiency'), i.e. 'why did want/poverty afflict his spirit?'. The clause asks for the cause of the soul's affliction.
segments.s654-g353: This is a verb‑first rhetorical clause: چه (interrogative 'what') + گوید (literary 3rd‑person singular of گفتن, here used as a present/future rhetorical 'will say/does say') followed by the subject. سخنگوی باآفرین is a noun phrase (سخنگو 'speaker/orator') modified by the epithet باآفرین ('with praise' / 'praise‑giving' — a poetic epithet); the epithet follows the noun as is normal in Persian poetry. The verb precedes its subject for emphasis, a common classical style.
segments.s654-g354: A series of prepositional phrases introduced by ز ('from/of', here 'concerning' or 'about') listing the persons spoken of: ز شاه ('about the king'), وز هیتال (here written as the conjunction و + preposition ز: 'and about Hital'), and خاقان چین ('the Khagan of China' — compound where خاقان is qualified by چین). The repeated ز marks each item as the topic/object of 'گوید'.
segments.s654-g81: که‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional (equivalent to اگر) meaning "if" and introduces the protasis. زو is the fused preposition ز (از) + او, meaning "from him/of him" and precedes the verb خورد, here خورد is the perfect/past-stem used in the sense "befall/strike" (classical usage: something 'befalls' someone or 'is struck'). بی‌گمان is an adverb "undoubtedly" modifying the clause; روی و سنگ are coordinated direct objects/affected things ("face and stone").
segments.s654-g82: بریزد is a present/subjunctive/indicative form of ریختن (here "fall/pour/are scattered") giving the result clause after the conditional; هم is emphatic "even/also" stressing immediacy. اندر زمان is a prepositional time phrase "in/at the time/instant," and بی‌درنگ is an adverb "immediately/without delay," which together indicate the action happens at once.
segments.s654-g83: نگه کرد is a simple past compound (gaze/look + کرد) meaning "looked"; classical Persian commonly places the verb before the subject, so زروان (Zurvān) is the actor. به گفتار اوی uses به to mark the target/recipient of the look ("at his speech/words"), and اوی is a possessive/third-person marker "his."
segments.s654-g84: دلش تازه‌تر شد is a past statement "his heart became fresher/newer," with -تر forming the comparative "more fresh." به دیدار اوی uses به to mark the occasion/cause ("upon/at the sight/meeting of him") and اوی again is the possessive/pronominal form "his," giving the causal sense "became renewed at seeing him."
segments.s654-g85: نرفتی is the negative past form (ن + رفت + -ی) meaning "did not go" (classical forms can reflect narrative person/number variations). به درگاه marks the destination "to the court/royal presence," and بی‌آن جهود is an adverbial phrase "without that Jew," with بی‌آن = "without that" and جهود here denoting "the Jew(s)" (used as an identifier).
segments.s654-g86: خور و شادی و کام is a triad of nouns (خور = "sun" in poetic usage, شادی = "joy," کام = "desire/fulfillment"). بی او نبود is a negative existential construction literally "was not without him," so the whole phrase means that sun, joy and satisfaction did not exist apart from him (i.e., they accompanied him). نبود is the past of بودن used existentially.
segments.s654-g87: چنین تا introduces a temporal clause "thus until..." and برآمد is a past verb "arose/occurred/was brought about." برین should be read as بر این (on/at this), an older fused form, and چندگاه means "for a while/after a few days," so together they form the temporal: "until this short time passed/arose."
segments.s654-g88: بد آموز here is an adjectival compound meaning "badly instructing/ill-instructed" (a critical epithet). پویان is a proper name (Puyan). به درگاه شاه uses به to mark motion toward the place "to the king's court." Word order places the epithet and name before the destination phrase.
segments.s654-g289: چنان کز is a compressed poetic conjunction meaning "such that" or "so that": چنان = "such" and کز (contracted که + از/که) introduces the subordinate clause. پس here means "after" and governs مرگ ("death"); نوشین‌روان is a compounded epithet placed after مرگ and describes the deceased as "sweet‑souled." The whole segment sets the temporal/causal frame: "such that after the death of the sweet‑souled (one)".
segments.s654-g290: ز گفتار من = "by/from my speech/word" (ز as a poetic preposition indicating source or cause, and من as the genitive "my"). داد is the simple past "gave/granted" used causatively, and it is followed by a result clause introduced by the verbless sequence او شد جوان where شد is the past of شدن "to become" and جوان is the predicate "young." Grammatically the line means the speaker's words effected/granted a result: "by my words (he was) granted — he became young."
segments.s654-g291: ازان پس که is a temporal phrase: ازان پس = "from that time/after that," and که introduces the subordinate clause. گیتی بدوگشت is literal "the world turned to him" (بدو‑گشت = turned/returned toward him), and راست functions adverbially as "rightly/favorably." Together: "From that time, when the world turned rightly toward him..."
segments.s654-g292: جز از = "except (from)" marks the only thing he desired. آفرین here means "praise/acclamation," در بزرگی = "in (his) greatness" (در as locative/in), and نخواست is the past negative/privative of خواستن meaning "he did not (want)" but with جز it conveys "he wanted nothing except..." — i.e. "he desired nothing in his greatness except praise."
segments.s654-g293: بخفتند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb (they slept/lay down). The prepositional phrase در دشت (in the plain) follows it; خرد و بزرگ is a coordinated noun phrase 'small and great' (i.e. the lowly and the great) that functions as the subject/affected parties of بخفتند. Word order here (verb before locative and then the nouns) is typical of poetic/narrative inversion.
segments.s654-g294: به + آبشخور is a motion-to phrase ('to the watering-place'), and آمد is the past verb 'came'. The particle همی is an archaic/narrative particle placed before the subject to give an ongoing or emphatic narrative flavour. میش وگرگ (sheep and wolf) is the subject that follows the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s654-g295: مهان is 'guests/visitors' and کهتری (here postpositive) qualifies them as 'the lesser/low-ranked'. را is the definite object marker marking that phrase as the object, and بیاراستند is the past 3rd-person plural verb 'they adorned/arrayed/prepared'. The whole segment means 'they arrayed the lower-ranked guests.'
segments.s654-g296: به دیهیم is a prepositional phrase meaning 'among/to the villagers' (diehīm here refers to rural people). بر نام او is a prepositional phrase 'with/on his name' functioning as the object of خواستند, and خواستند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they desired/wanted/sought'. Together it reads 'they sought a name for him among the villagers' (i.e. they tried to give or establish his name/reputation in the village context).
segments.s654-g297: ز introduces a source or the thing being freed ('from'); گردن is the bodily noun 'neck' and is the thing being referred to; بیاسود functions as a predicate/adjective describing the state (here 'not at ease / restless' but in this clause it carries the sense of being released from restraint), so the phrase order is subject/thing (گردن) followed by the descriptive predicate (بیاسود) and then the prepositional phrase ز بند زره 'from the strap of the armor' specifying what the neck is freed from. بند is used in the concrete sense 'strap/fetter' and زره 'armor'.
segments.s654-g298: ز جوشن marks the thing from which an action proceeds ('from the cuirass'); گشادند is a past plural verb meaning 'opened/loosened' (here an active past, used with the plural subject گردان 'ranks/troops'); the verb comes after its complements in this poetic word order. گره is the object 'knot/clasp' that the گردان loosened.
segments.s654-g299: ز here can introduce an origin or association ('from' / 'concerning') and is followed by the coordinated nouns کوپال و خنجر (Koopal and the dagger) as the topic; بیاسود is the predicate meaning 'uneasy/restless' and دوش is a temporal adverb 'last night' placed at the end for poetic emphasis. The order is topic → predicate → temporal adjunct, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s654-g300: جز = 'except' introduces the sole item that did reach the ear; آواز رامش is a noun phrase 'the sound/voice of calm' (آواز modified by رامش), and نیامد is the past negative 'did not come' — with به گوش ('to the ear') the idiom means 'no sound reached the ear except...'. Word order places the exception (جز ...) at the start for emphasis.
segments.s654-g301: کسی را uses the object/topic marker را with کسی; نبد is an archaic negative copular/verb meaning 'was not / there was not', so the clause reads roughly 'there was no one (کسی را نبد) with the sovereign's تاو (radiance)'. با before جهاندار links the comparison agent ('with the sovereign'), and تاو is a nominal 'radiance/glow' that gevels the quality being compared. The order places the compared item (کسی) first, then the negative verb, then the comparator.
segments.s654-g302: بپیوست is a past verb 'joined' or 'was attached' (here a simple past active meaning 'joined'); با هرکسی is the prepositional phrase 'with every/any man' indicating accompaniment; باژ و ساو are two coordinated nouns following that phrase — in context they function as names or terms grouped with 'every man' (the conjunction و links them). The clause thus states the joining/association with others, with the coordinated nouns as the partners.
segments.s654-g303: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'; دشواری is the direct object 'difficulty/hardship'; آسان here functions as a resultative/adverbial modifier 'easy' and together with گرفت (past 'took' / 'made') forms the idiom 'آسان گرفت' = 'made easy' or 'relieved/made light of'. The verb گرفت is simple past active agreeing with the subject.
segments.s654-g304: همه modifies the following noun phrase as 'all/every'; ساز is a concrete noun 'instrument' (here likely 'instruments' or 'means'); نخچیر و میدان are coordinated nouns 'spoil/booty' and 'field/arena' respectively; گرفت repeats the past verb 'took/seized'. Word order lists the items taken and finishes with the past verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s654-g233: This is an indirect question/command clause. که here functions as the complementizer introducing the content ('that'/'how'), این کار 'this affair' is the object, چون بود = 'how it was' (چون = 'how', بود = past 'was'), and با من بگوی is an imperative/requestal use meaning 'tell (me)' (literally 'say with me' — classical word order places the clause before the verb).
segments.s654-g234: A short admonition. بدست here means 'by/through' or 'by means of'; دروغ = 'falsehood/lie'; ایچ is an emphatic negation 'not at all'/'do not'; منمای (imperative/negative form) = 'do not show' (me); روی = 'face' (literal or figurative face/appearance). The clause is ordered as instrument + object + negative command directed at the addressee.
segments.s654-g235: Subject + prepositional phrase + object + verb: جهود here is a proper/ethnic name (classical usage), از جهاندار = 'from the sovereign', زنهار is a noun 'warning/admonition', and خواست is the past verb 'asked/requested'. The order places the requester first, then source, then the requested thing, then the verb.
segments.s654-g236: Complement clause introduced by که 'that/so that' expressing purpose/result: پیداکند is subjunctive/present-form of the verb 'to find/discover' ('that he might find'), راز = 'secret', نیرنگ = 'deception/trick', and راست qualifies نیرنگ as 'true/real' (here meaning the actual nature of the deceit).
segments.s654-g237: بگفت is a past-tense verb 'he said/told'; آنچ = 'that which/what'; زروان is the proper name Zurvān; بدو = 'to him'; گفته بود = past perfect 'had said' (گفته = past participle, بود = auxiliary past). The clause reports reported speech: 'he said what Zurvān had told him.'
segments.s654-g238: Syntactically this is a comment on the words: سخن هرچ = 'every/whatever word', اندر = 'in/within', نهان = 'hidden/secret', رفته بود = past perfect 'had gone' (resultative of being hidden). Together: 'every word had gone into concealment.'
segments.s654-g239: جهاندار (the sovereign) is the subject, بشنید is past tense 'he heard', and خیره بماند is a compound predicate meaning 'he remained stunned/was left staring' (خیره = 'astonished/staring', بماند = 'remained'). The sequence gives reaction after hearing.
segments.s654-g240: An object clause: names are given and then the verb بخواند ('he called/summoned') — مرزبان را is marked with را as the definite/direct object. The conjunction و links the listed officials. In classical narrative, the list comes before the verb.
segments.s654-g97: زدست is a prepositional phrase meaning “by/from the hand of” placed before the noun phrase; دو فرزند is the counted noun phrase “two sons/children”; آن ارجمند is a descriptive phrase “that noble (one), the esteemed” modifying فرزند — Ferdowsi omits the ezāfe link that modern Persian would show, so the sequence literally reads “two sons of that noble.”
segments.s654-g98: رسیدی is a past/perfective verb meaning “reached/arrived” (Ferdowsi sometimes uses older verb endings; here it functions as the main past action). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase “to/into the proximity of” and takes its object after it; شاه بلند is a noun plus epithet “the lofty/high king,” with the adjective following the noun as is common in Persian epic style.
segments.s654-g99: خورشها is the plural of خورش “dishes/served foods.” The following list zِشهد و ز شیر و گلاب enumerates ingredients/flavors — ز here is the old preposition ‘from/of’ and is combined with the copulative و (so written as وز in the edition), yielding “of honey and of milk and rosewater.” This is a poetic list of what the dishes contained.
segments.s654-g100: بخوردی is a past/perfective verbal form meaning “they partook/ate” (classical Persian verb morphology can differ from modern forms; the narrator indicates the action of eating). The prefixed و in و آراستی joins the next verb: آراستی “(you/they) adorned/arranged” (here plural agents are implied). جای خواب is a compound “place of sleep” = sleeping place/bed, the direct object of آراستن in context.
segments.s654-g101: چنان بد که is a resultive expression “so (much/thus) that” — here بد functions as an intensifier in the classical idiom, and که introduces the resulting clause. یک روز gives the time “one day,” and هر دو جوان is the subject “both young men,” where هر both/each precedes the noun.
segments.s654-g102: ببردند is a past, 3rd‑person plural verb “they carried/took.” خوان is the direct object “the banquet/feast.” نزدنوشین‌روان is a locative/goal expression meaning “to/near the sweet‑souled one” (نزد + the epithet نوشین‌روان), indicating the destination/recipient of the action.
segments.s654-g103: به سر برنهاده is a participial/prepositional phrase literally “having set on the head” or idiomatically “appointed as head/chief”; به introduces the locative/goal, سر is “head,” and برنهاده is a past participial verbal adjective “placed/put.” یکی پیشکار is “a certain attendant” (یکی functions as an indefinite quantifier ‘one / a certain’ modifying پیشکار).
segments.s654-g104: که introduces a relative clause modifying پیشکار (“who…”). بودی is the past form of بودن “was” (classical past third‑person), here introducing the description. خورش نزد او استوار describes the attendant’s role: خورش “the dishes,” نزد “near/in the presence of/at the side of,” او “him” (the master/king), and استوار “steady/firm” — together: “who kept the dishes steady beside him.”
segments.s654-g113: جوان is the subject ('the young man'). زان خورش is a prepositional/relational phrase 'of/from that stew' (زان = 'from that', خورش = 'stew'). زود بگشاد is a past action: زود ('quickly') modifies the verb بگشاد (the old/persianate past form of گشودن, 'opened/uncovered'); روی is the object meaning 'surface/face' (here 'the stew's surface or lid'). Word order is subject — modifying phrase — adverb — verb — object, typical of the epic style.
segments.s654-g114: نگه کرد is a light verb construction meaning 'gazed/looked' (نگه = 'gaze' + کرد past 'did/kept'); زروان is the subject here (the name Zurvān). ز دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'. اند is an archaic/poetic element (an enclitic form appearing in classical Persian poetry) that does not change the basic sense here, and روی ('face') indicates the target/direction of the gaze — 'he looked toward (with his face)'. The whole segment therefore reads 'Zurvān looked from afar.'
segments.s654-g115: همیدون is a proper/name functioning as subject. جهود is the direct object here (an archaic plural often used for 'Jews' in classical Persian). اندرو (= اندر + او) is a prepositional/locative phrase 'inside it' / 'within', and بنگرید is a classical/past-looking form of نگریستن ('looked' or 'gazed'); here it reports that he 'looked inside.' The verb has the classical prefix ب‑ which is common in older Persian perfect/past forms.
segments.s654-g116: پس آمد = 'then he came' (پس = 'then/afterward', آمد = past 'came/approached'). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when': چو رنگ خورشها بدید = 'when he saw the color/appearance of the stews' (رنگ here = 'appearance/complexion', خورشها = plural 'the stews', بدید = past 'saw'). The subordinate clause follows the main verb, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s654-g117: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past of گفتن 'said'). زان پس is a temporal/ordinal marker 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'then/afterwards'). به سالار بار is an indirect object phrase 'to the master/commander of the load' (سالار = 'chief/commander', بار = 'load/cargo'), i.e. 'he said to the cargo‑master.'
segments.s654-g118: This is a subordinate clause: که آمد درختی = 'that there came a tree' (که = 'that', آمد = 'came', درختی = indefinite 'a tree'); the second که introduces a relative clause modifying درختی: که کشتی به بار = 'which the ship (took) as cargo' — the verb is elliptical/understood in the compressed poetic diction, so 'کشتی به بار' functions like 'the ship for cargo' or 'which was put aboard the ship.'
segments.s654-g119: ببردند is the 3rd‑person plural past of بردن with the classical prefix ب‑, 'they carried/took'. خوان is the direct object 'banquet/table'. نزد + نوشین‌روان is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of Nushin‑ravan' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of'), so the clause means 'they carried the banquet to Nushin‑ravan.'
segments.s654-g120: This is a verbless descriptive phrase: خردمند و بیدار are coordinated adjectives 'wise and alert/awake'. هر دو جوان = 'both young (men)' — هر دو is an idiom meaning 'both', placed before the noun جوان ('youths/young men'). Together they describe the two youths who are wise and alert.
segments.s654-g273: وگر introduces a conditional ('if'). چند here is an adverbial modifier meaning 'somewhat' or 'a little' modifying نرمست, which is a predicative adjective ('is soft/gentle') attached to آواز تو ('your voice'); word order is ornamental: 'چند نرمست آواز تو' = 'your voice is somewhat soft'.
segments.s654-g274: گشاده شود is a passive/impersonal verbal phrase ('will be opened / will become open'); ز + و here functions as the classical contracted preposition+pronoun ('از او' = 'from it/thereby'), so ز + و + همه راز تو together mean 'from it all your secret(s)'; the object (همه راز تو) follows the verb in poetic order.
segments.s654-g275: ندارد is the negative present of داشتن ('does not have / there is not'); نگه here means 'keeping' (i.e., retention) rather than 'gaze'; راز مردم زبان is a possessive/genitive grouping where زبان (speech/tongue) qualifies رازِ مردم ('the people's secrets/speech'): overall, 'the people's tongue does not keep secret' (i.e., public talk does not keep secrets).
segments.s654-g276: همان به که is an idiomatic clause meaning roughly 'it is as well / it is best that'; به functions as a preposition introducing the following clause, and که introduces the subordinate clause with نیکی کنی in the present/subjunctive sense ('that you do good'); درجهان here is an adverbial/locative phrase ('in the world').
segments.s654-g277: چو is a conditional/subordinate conjunction here ('if'/'when'); بیرنج is an adjectival phrase 'without grief' modifying the implied subject 'you'; باشی is the present subjunctive/optative of بودن used for the protasis ('if you are'); پاکیزه‌رای is a coordinating predicate ('and of pure mind'), so the clause reads 'if you are free from grief and of pure disposition'.
segments.s654-g278: ازو ('از' + pronoun) means 'from it/from that'; بهره یابی is a verbal phrase 'you will obtain/attain a share/benefit' with یابی as present/subjunctive/indicative depending on context but here future sense; به هر دو سرای is a prepositional phrase 'in both abodes/realms' (i.e., this world and the next), placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s654-g279: کنون is an adverb 'now'; کار زروان is a genitive phrase 'the work/affair of Zurvān' (Zurvān = personified Time); و links the two nouns, and مرد جهود here means 'the man of effort' or collectively 'men's efforts' (جهود used in the sense 'efforts'). The sequence lists items/theme before the main clause.
segments.s654-g280: سرآمد خرد is a nominal phrase 'the foremost (or chief) of wisdom'; را is the object marker attaching emphasis/definiteness to that phrase; بباید is the impersonal modal 'one must/it is necessary to' and ستود is the verb 'to praise' in base form, so the clause means 'the foremost of wisdom must be praised.'
segments.s654-g337: یکی ... و دگر introduces two contrasted individuals: یکی 'one' (here 'one person') followed by پیشه کار 'craftsman' ('پیشه' = profession, 'کار' = work), و دگر 'and another', then کشت ورز is a compound meaning 'one who cultivates / a tiller' (کشت = cultivation, ورز from 'ورزیدن' = to practice). The verbs are omitted—this is a verbless nominal listing typical in Persian: 'one [was] a craftsman and another [was] a farmer.'
segments.s654-g338: یکی 'one' is resumed and آنک is a relative/connecting particle meaning 'who' or 'the one who'. پیمود is the past 3rd-person singular verb 'traversed / covered', and فرسنگ و مرز are the objects ('parasangs/far distances and borders'). The clause means 'one (was) the one who traversed parasangs and borders'—a relative clause modifying that one person.
segments.s654-g339: The correlative 'چه ... و چه ...' enumerates types (English 'whether... or.../be it... or...'). Here the first 'چه' introduces 'بازارگان' (merchant/trader) and the second introduces the contrasting class. This is not an interrogative; it lists two social/religious categories. Word order is simple noun phrase (no verb) because this line pairs with the following balanced clause.
segments.s654-g340: 'یکی' again marks one group/person. 'سرفراز' is an adjective/noun meaning proud/exalted or honorable. 'و دگر' links the second contrasting description: 'دگر' = 'another/other'. 'زیردست' is an adjective/noun meaning subordinate/underling. This is a parallel nominal construction ('one [is] exalted and another [is] lowly') with the copula omitted in classical Persian.
segments.s654-g341: 'بیاراست' is a past tense / perfective form from آراستن with the prefix بی- (classical usage) meaning 'he adorned' or 'was well adorned' depending on voice—here active: 'he adorned'. 'آن شارستان' = 'that city/town' (آن = that, شارستان = town). 'چون' functions as a simile marker 'like' followed by 'بهشت' (paradise). The phrase literally reads 'He adorned that town like paradise.'
segments.s654-g342: This is a negative past clause: 'ندید' = 'did not see' (ن- negative prefix + دید past). 'اندرو' = 'in it/within' (اندر + او), placing the location before the noun. 'چشم' here functions as 'the eye/sight' (subject/experiencer). 'یک جای زشت' = 'a single ugly place' (یک = one, جای = place, زشت = ugly). The literal order is 'did not see in it the eye a single ugly place' — natural English: 'within it the eye did not see a single ugly spot.'
segments.s654-g343: 'ورا' is the enclitic/formal object or locative meaning 'to/for it' (ورا = 'to it' or 'there'). 'سورستان' is a compound/name meaning a place of feasts or a feast-ground; here treated as the noun given to the place. 'کرد' is the past tense of کردن 'to make/do' = 'made'. 'کسری' is the subject (the king Khosrow). 'به نام' = 'by the name' or 'in the name of' — 'به' is a preposition and 'نام' means 'name'. Together: 'Khosrow made it Suristan in name' (i.e. named it Suristan or made it a place called Suristan).
segments.s654-g344: 'که' here introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). 'درسور' = 'dar + sur' = 'at the feast' (در = in/at + سور = feast). 'یابد' is the present/subjunctive form of یافتن used here purposively meaning 'may obtain' or 'might attain' (classical Persian uses the simple present in such subordinate clauses). 'جهاندار' = 'the sovereign/king' (object/beneficiary), and 'کام' = 'desire/satisfaction'. So the clause means 'so that at the feast the sovereign may obtain satisfaction.'
segments.s654-g185: بدو (bedo) is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past 3rd-person singular 'said' (poetic word order places the object/indirect-object before or around the verb); زروان is the subject (Zurvān, here the speaker) following the verb; انوشه is a proper name (vocative/quoted addressee) and بدی is a noun 'evil' attached to that name as the opening words of the reported content. In short: 'بدو گفت زروان انوشه بدی' = 'Zurvān said to him: “Anoushe, (you have) evil/ill…”' (poetic inversions place 'to him' before the verb and the speaker-name after it).
segments.s654-g186: خرد را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker (خرد = 'wisdom'); به گفتار is an adverbial phrase with به meaning 'by/with' and گفتار 'speech/words'; توشه 'provisions/store' is the noun receiving the qualifier; بدی 'evil' is the quality of that store. The verb that completes the idea is deferred to the next clause in the poetic line (i.e. 'you have furnished Wisdom with an evil store by your words').
segments.s654-g187: ز جادو is a poetic prepositional phrase 'about/from magic' (ز = 'from/about'); سخن هرچ = 'whatever speech/words' (هرچ 'whatever' modifies سخن); گویند is the impersonal/present plural used like an impersonal 'they say' or passive 'is said'; هست is the copula 'is' placed at the end for emphasis. Together: 'Whatever words are said about magic (exist/are such)'.
segments.s654-g188: نداند is the classical negative form 'knows not' (ن‑ + داند); جز (here) = 'except'; از introduces the agent/source ('from/by'); مرد جادوپرست is the restricting phrase 'the man who worships/serves magic' (i.e. the magician). Word order is straightforward: 'no one knows except from/by a magician.'
segments.s654-g189: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; خوردنی is used as a noun/adjective meaning 'edible/food'; دارد is the present/prehistoric-form 'has/contains'; از شیر is a prepositional phrase 'from the lion' (شیر here = 'lion' in this epic context); بهر = 'for' or 'for the purpose of' (introducing the intended effect). The clause sets a condition: 'If it has any edible (thing) from the lion for ...'.
segments.s654-g190: پدیدار here functions as 'manifest/appearance' governing the causative verb گرداند ('causes/makes appear'); از دور is the adverbial 'from afar'; زهر is the patient 'poison/venom'. The verb گرداند is causative 'cause to appear' so the whole clause reads 'it causes poison to appear from afar.'
segments.s654-g191: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past 3rd-person 'he heard'; نوشین‌روان is a proper epithet/name ('sweet‑soul', here the person who hears); این سخن 'these words/this speech' is the object. The sequence 'چو بشنید X این سخن' = 'when X heard these words.'
segments.s654-g192: برو here functions as a discourse particle meaning 'thereupon/then' (poetic usage) rather than the literal imperative 'go'; تازه 'new/fresh'; شد is the past 3rd-person 'became'; روزگار کهن 'the old time/age' (روزگار = time/age, کهن = old). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'thereupon the old age became fresh.'
segments.s654-g225: گنه یک‌سر افگند سوی جهود: گنه (sin/guilt) is the object/topic; یک‌سر (here expressed as سر with یک before it) functions adverbially as 'utterly/completely'. افگند is past simple 'threw/cast'. سوی + efforts/name (جهود) marks direction/target 'toward the Jews' (جهود used in the archaic sense 'Jews'). Word order is typical poetry: object/topic (گنه) + manner/adverb (یک‌سر) + verb (افگند) + direction (سوی جهود).
segments.s654-g226: تن خویش را کرد پر درد و دود: تن خویش (his own body) is the direct object, marked by the object marker را. کرد is the past tense 'made/caused' (he caused), and پر followed by nouns is an adjectival phrase 'full (of)'. درد و دود are the two nouns filling 'پر' ('full of pain and smoke'). The construction literally: 'he made his body full of pain and smoke.'
segments.s654-g227: چو بشنید زو شهریار بلند: چو + past verb بشنید = 'when he heard'. ز (poetic 'from/of') + (the following pronoun element written as و) = 'of/from him/it'. شهریار بلند is the noun phrase 'the lofty/exalted king' serving as subject/topic. This is a typical temporal clause ('When the lofty king heard of it...').
segments.s654-g228: هم اندر زمان پای کردش ببند: هم اندر زمان = 'at the same time / immediately'; پای here 'foot' used in the idiom of binding the feet (i.e. to bind someone). کردش is 'he caused/did to him' (کرد + object clitic ش = 'had him ...'), and ببند (infinitive/imperative-formed stem) together with کردش yields the causative 'had him bound'. So: 'he at once had him bound.'
segments.s654-g229: فرستاد نزد مشعبد جهود: فرستاد is simple past 'sent'. نزد marks 'to / in the presence of' and مشعبد appears to be a proper name or title (here treated as a place/person 'Mashʿabd'). جهود in this text is the archaic plural 'the Jews' and functions as the indirect object/recipient of the sending. Word order: verb + to/near + addressee + recipient.
segments.s654-g230: دواسبه سواری به کردار دود: دواسبه is 'two-horse / a pair of horses' (compound numeral+horse), سواری 'riders/horsemen'; به کردار = 'in the manner/form of', and دود 'smoke'. The whole phrase describes what was sent: 'two mounted horsemen on a pair of horses, in the guise/appearance of smoke.'
segments.s654-g231: چوآمد بدان بارگاه بلند: چوآمد 'when he came / when he arrived' (چو + آمد fused in poetry). بدان is a poetic prepositional target 'to that' (ب + دان/‌آن), بارگاه بلند 'that lofty court'. The clause means 'when he came to that lofty court.'
segments.s654-g232: بپرسید زو نرم شاه بلند: بپرسید is past 'asked/questioned'; ز (from/of) + the pronoun element (و) = 'of him/it' (i.e. 'asked him'). نرم here is an adverb/adjective 'softly/gently'. شاه بلند 'the lofty/exalted king' is the agent; the order places the verb first as is common in narrative poetry, so: 'the lofty king gently asked him.'
segments.s654-g41: همی‌ساختی is an archaic/poetic imperfective meaning “kept making/contriving”; the prefix همی marks continuous or habitual action. تا introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that”); سر پادشا literally “the king’s head” = the king’s mind/attention (Persian uses سر metaphorically for mind).
segments.s654-g42: کند is the 3rd-person form of کردن used here with تا to express the intended/resulting action (“would make” or “make” in a purpose sense). تیز describes the resulting quality (“keen/acute”); برکار is a compound meaning “toward/about the work/deeds” and links what the king becomes keen about. آن پارسا is a demonstrative + epithet: “that pious man.”
segments.s654-g43: ببد here is a proper name (a speaker), so it functions as subject; گفت is the past of گفتن “said.” از ایشان means “in/of them” or “from those people,” and ندید ایچ راه is a negated past “(he) saw no way at all” (ایچ = nothing/at all, راه = way/method).
segments.s654-g44: که introduces the content clause (‘that’). کردی is the past tense ‘you did’ (direct accusation). پرآزار qualifies the harm as severe/troublesome. زان here links to the following noun phrase as a deictic ‘that/of that’; جان شاه = “the king’s life/soul.” The whole clause says what harm was alleged.
segments.s654-g45: خردمند is the subject ‘the wise man.’ زان بد = ‘of that evil / of that mischief’ (زان = ‘of/that’ + بد = ‘bad/evil’). نه آگاه بود is a negated past copular clause ‘was not aware’ (نه negates آگاه بود).
segments.s654-g46: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause (‘that’). او را is the accusative/topic-marked pronoun ‘him.’ به درگاه = ‘at/to the court/presence’ (به + درگاه). بدخواه بود = ‘there was an ill‑wisher/hostile person’; بود is the past copula. The clause explains what the wise man did not know.
segments.s654-g47: ز is the poetic form of از meaning ‘from/because of.’ گفتار و کردار = ‘speech and conduct’ (two parallel nouns). آن شوخ مرد = ‘that playful/witty man’ (آن = that; شوخ here = playful/witty/jesting). The whole phrase gives the cause/source.
segments.s654-g48: نشد is the negated past of شدن ‘became/did become’ (here ‘did not become’). هیچ intensifies the negation (‘not at all’ / ‘no’). مهبود is an epithet (literally ‘moon‑like one’ — often a beloved or radiant person) and را marks it as the grammatical object; روی زرد = ‘face (become) pale.’ Together: ‘the moon‑like one’s face did not become pale at all.’
segments.s654-g217: چو introduces a temporal clause (‘when’). پاسخ ازو is a noun + prepositional phrase 'the reply from him'. لرز لرزان is a doubled adjective/nominal phrase qualifying how the reply was given — 'trembling' — placed before the verb. شنید is the simple past 'he heard', closing the clause.
segments.s654-g218: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from'. زروان names the source (Zurvān). گنهکاری functions as the subject ('sinfulness/guilt' or 'a sinful state'). آمد پدید is a two-word verbal phrase 'came forth/appeared' (آمد + پدید, literal 'came, appeared').
segments.s654-g219: بدو is dative 'to him' and precedes the verb for emphasis. گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (the provided sense list lacks the verb sense, see senses). کسری is the speaker 'Kisra'. سخن راست گوی is an instruction: سخن راست (true speech) + گوی, an archaic/poetic present/imperative form of 'say' — 'speak the truth.'
segments.s654-g220: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not'. کژی names the wrongdoing to be avoided ('crookedness', 'deceit'). و links the two prohibitions. هیچ چاره مجوی is an imperative negative: هیچ 'no/any' + چاره 'remedy/means' + مجوی 'do not seek' ( مجوی = negative imperative/ jussive of جستن).
segments.s654-g221: که introduces an explanatory/reason clause ('for' / 'because'). کژی 'crookedness' is the subject. نیارد here is an older literary negative-verb form meaning 'does not bring' (classical نیاورد / نمی‌آورد) — the provided senses do not include this verbal sense. مگر 'except' introduces the exception clause: 'brings nothing except bad deed/result' (کار بد).
segments.s654-g222: دل نیک 'a good heart' is the subject phrase. بد گردد uses گردد (گردیدن) in the sense 'becomes' (گردد = becomes). از marks cause/agent 'from' or 'through' and یار بد 'a bad companion' gives the reason: 'a good heart becomes bad because of a bad friend.'
segments.s654-g223: سراسر modifies سخن راست: 'the whole/entire true speech'. زروان is the speaker. بگفت is the past-tense narrative verb 'he said/spoke' (archaic/poetic b-prefixed past).
segments.s654-g224: نهفته is 'the hidden' (object). پدید آورید is an imperative plural/optative 'make appear' or 'bring forth' (پدید + آورید, with آورید as imperative 'bring' / 'cause to be'). از نهفت adds 'from hiding/from concealment' — a prepositional phrase marking source. The clause is a command: 'Bring out the hidden from concealment.'
segments.s654-g89: This is a noun phrase: دو (implied numeral) + فرزند + مهبود (a name or epithet) = "the two children of Mahbud." Classical Persian often omits the ezafe vowel, so مهبود is a genitive/attributive after فرزند. هر بامداد follows as a temporal adverbial meaning "each/ every morning."
segments.s654-g90: خرامان is an adverbial/participial description "with a stately/proud gait." شدندی is an archaic past plural form meaning "they became / they came to be" (here idiomatically: "they came/approached in that manner"). برشاه is a prepositional phrase "before/to the king" (again ezafe is omitted), and راد is an adjective (or honorific epithet) modifying شاه meaning "generous/noble."
segments.s654-g91: پس here has a locational/relational force ("after/behind") and heads the phrase پس پرده نامور کدخدای = "behind the famed curtain of the kadkhoda." Classical Persian poetry often drops the ezafe between nouns, so پرده نامور کدخدای reads as "the renowned curtain of the kadkhoda."
segments.s654-g92: زنی بود is a simple past copular clause: زنی "a woman" + بود "was." The postpositive adjectives پاکیزه و پاک رای form the predicate: پاکیزه "chaste/clean in conduct" and پاک رای literally "pure in counsel/opinion." Note the coordination with و linking the two qualities.
segments.s654-g93: This subordinate clause begins with که introducing a reason/description ("that/for"), then چون in its temporal sense (here: "when/just as") followed by شاه کسری ("King Khosrow") and خورش "his cooked dish/stew." خواستی is the past-tense verb "desired/wished for." Together they mean "that when King Khosrow desired his stew..."
segments.s654-g94: یکی خوان is a noun phrase "a certain/one spread/table (خوان = meal/banquet)." زرین is an adjective "golden." The final word بیاراستی is an older/perhaps dialectal verb form meaning "arrayed/arranged/laid out" (here: "set out"); the printed sense choices given do not fit perfectly, so this item is treated as an archaic verbal form meaning "prepared/arrayed."
segments.s654-g95: سه کاسه is a simple numeral + noun "three bowls." نهادی here is an archaic past form of نهادن (to put/place) agreeing with the feminine subject established earlier ("she placed"). برو is an older locative/adverb meaning roughly "there/for him/at that place" and از گهر attaches to indicate contents "from/of gems" producing "three bowls of gems." (Some of these forms are archaic and the modern dictionary senses supplied do not line up exactly.)
segments.s654-g96: به marks the locative/attendant relation here ("on/at/with"), دستار "turban" is the noun, modified by زربفت "gold-brocaded." پوشیده is a participle/adjectival past "covered/worn," and سر is "head." The phrase reads literally "with a gold-brocaded turban covering the head," i.e. "and on her head was a gold-brocaded turban."
segments.s654-g153: 'بگشت' here is a narrative past meaning 'turned/changed' (a poetic verb placed before its complements). 'اندرین نیز' = 'in this too/also' (اندرین = 'in this/among these', نیز = 'also') and 'چندی' gives the duration 'for a while' placed before the noun سپهر ('heavens'), so the phrase reads 'for a while the heavens changed in this too.'
segments.s654-g154: درستی ('righteousness / honesty') is the direct object; نهان کرده is a perfect participle construction with سپهر (the heavens) as its understood subject: 'the heavens have hidden / hid.' از شاه چهر is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's face' (از = 'from', شاه = 'king', چهر = 'face'), so the clause means 'they hid righteousness from the king's face.'
segments.s654-g155: چنان بد که is a fixed result clause 'so/so bad that...' introducing the outcome. شاه جهان is a title-noun 'the king of the world' (شاه + جهان). کدخدای follows as the noun affected by the result: the phrase sets up that even the king was reduced to the role of a کدخدای (village headman) or acted as one.
segments.s654-g156: به introduces the target/concern ('about/for'); نخچیر here means 'game/prey' (the context of hunting), and گوران is read as the ethnonym/group (the Guran) or their domain, so 'the Guran's game.' همی‌کرد is an imperfect/habitual past 'would do / was doing' and رای here means 'counsel/decision' — together 'همی‌کرد رای' means 'would take/hold counsel' or 'would deliberate/decide.'
segments.s654-g157: بفرمود is a courtly past tense 'he ordered/commanded.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). اسب نخچیرگاه is a genitive phrase 'horses of the hunting-ground' (اسب = 'horse', نخچیرگاه = 'hunting-ground'), the direct object of the following verb.
segments.s654-g158: بسی is a quantifier 'many/much' (classical usage; here modifying the following verb). بگذرانند is a subjunctive/imperative clause-form meaning 'they should cause to pass / lead past.' در پیش شاه is a locative phrase 'in front of / in the presence of the king' (در + پیش + شاه), completing the purpose: 'bring many (horses) past before the king.'
segments.s654-g159: ز اسبان = 'from the horses' (ز = 'from', اسبان = 'horses'). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; کسری is the proper name Khosrow (the king) and همی‌بنگرید is the imperfect progressive 'was looking at / was viewing' — together 'of the horses which Khosrow was looking at.'
segments.s654-g160: یکی را = 'one (of them)' with را as the definite object marker. بران is a fused form of بر آن 'on it/upon that' functioning as a locative particle. داغ مهبود is a genitive phrase 'the brand/mark of Mahbud' (داغ = 'brand/mark'), and دید is the past tense 'saw' — so the clause reads 'he saw one with Mahbud's brand on it.'
segments.s654-g329: This clause is a compact past‑tense clause: وزین is a poetic/contracted sequence (و + زین) meaning 'and from these/of these' introducing the source; هر یکی 'each one' (هر modifies یکی); را is the definite object marker attached to the noun phrase; یکی خانه کرد is 'he made one house' with کرد (past 3rd sg) as the main verb and خانه the direct object. Word order is typical classical Persian: (source) + (object marked by را) + verb.
segments.s654-g330: همه شارستان functions as a noun phrase 'the whole/entire town' (همه modifying شارستان). جای بیگانه کرد is a small resultative phrase: جای 'place' + بیگانه 'stranger/alien' (here 'of strangers' or 'alien') and کرد 'made/turned (into)'. The verb کرد takes the preceding noun phrase as its object: 'he made the whole town a place of strangers.'
segments.s654-g331: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. از شهر 'from the city' is a prepositional phrase. یک سر here is an adverbial expression meaning 'as one / all together' (idiomatic), and بپرداختند is the past plural verb agreeing with the subject 'they' — the whole clause: 'when they all departed from the city.'
segments.s654-g332: بگرد اندرش روستا ساختند is parsed as 'بگرد' = 'around' (poetic contraction of به گرد), اندر + ش = 'in/within it' (ش = enclitic 'it'), روستا 'villages', and ساختند past 3rd‑pl 'they built'. Word order places the locative phrase before the verb: 'around it they built villages.'
segments.s654-g333: بیاراست is a past/telic verb 'he adorned/arranged' (here 'he set out' or 'he planted/arranged'); بر هر سویی is a locative phrase 'on every side' with بر + هر سویی, and کشتزار is the object 'croplands/fields'. The clause therefore means 'he arranged cropland on every side.'
segments.s654-g334: This is a nominal/descriptive clause: زمین 'the land' is described by two adjectives linked by و هم: برومند 'prosperous/fertile' and میوه‌دار 'fruit‑bearing'. The coordinating و هم places emphasis 'and also/likewise', so 'the land was fertile and fruitful.'
segments.s654-g335: ازین هریکی را is 'from these (of them) to each one' (ازین = از این, هریکی = each one) with را marking the definite object/recipient; یکی کار داد is 'he gave one task/job' (یکی modifying کار). The clause is an SOV pattern: indirect object/recipient phrase + direct object + verb (داد 'gave').
segments.s654-g336: چوتنها is a fused adverb 'چو تنها' = 'when (he) was alone' or 'if alone'; بد 'bad' modifies the following noun phrase; از کارگر یار داد literally 'he gave a companion instead of/from the workers' — here meaning 'he gave him a poor/incompetent workmate.' داد is again the past 'gave'. The clause expresses an exception/qualification to the previous distribution.
segments.s654-g249: This is a compact, poetic verb‑object sequence where the verb بباران (repeated for emphasis) governs the following noun: بباران سنگ و بباران تیر = 'they caused stones to fall / they showered stones and they showered arrows.' The repeated بباران is a poetic causative/denominative use meaning 'to make X fall/shower' and the direct object (سنگ, تیر) follows the verb in standard Persian order.
segments.s654-g250: بدادند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense 'they gave/handed over.' سرها is the definite plural object 'the heads.' به introduces the indirect object/goal ('to' or 'into') and نیرنگ شیر is a genitive/compositional phrase 'the lion's stratagem/trick' (noun + noun). Together: 'they handed the heads over to the lion's stratagem.'
segments.s654-g251: جهان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the world' as the thing being spoken of. نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'it should not / one must not,' followed by the infinitive سپردن 'to entrust.' The final ببد is an archaic/past auxiliary form of بودن used here in epic diction to complete the clause ('it was not right to entrust' / 'one should not entrust').
segments.s654-g252: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for / because'). بر here functions as a prepositional particle 'on/upon' (roughly 'from' or 'because of' in this idiom) and combines with بد گمان 'bad suspicion' (گمان = suspicion). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' placed before the verb for emphasis, and بد رسد = 'evil will befall' where رسد (present/future sense) denotes occurrence/becoming.
segments.s654-g253: This line shows classical/poetic word order: ز (poetic از) 'from/among' + خویشان 'relatives' followed by the subject مهبود (a proper name) and then چندی 'for a while' and the past verb بجست 'he searched/sought.' In plain order: مهبود از خویشان چندی بجست = 'Mehbud searched among his relatives for a while.'
segments.s654-g254: کزیشان is a fused/poetic form meaning 'from among them' (کز = از + that/relative + ایشان). بیابد is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive/potential 'may find' used to express the hoped‑for result, followed by کسی 'someone' and the adjective تندرست 'healthy' as the predicate of that hoped‑for person: 'that he might find someone healthy among them.'
segments.s654-g255: یکی is an indefinite article/quantifier 'a certain' or 'one (of),' followed by دختری 'a girl' and the past verb یافت 'he found' (3rd singular). پوشیده‌روی is a postposed adjective 'modest‑faced / veiled‑faced' qualifying دختری, the normal adjective‑after‑noun order in Persian poetry.
segments.s654-g256: سه مرد is a simple noun phrase 'three men' and the following adjectives گرانمایه و نیک‌خوی ('esteemed' and 'good‑natured/virtuous') modify that phrase; adjectives typically follow the noun and are linked by و 'and.' The whole segment lists these additional people found.
segments.s654-g321: ز ... روم ... وز هند: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; here the repeated وز is the fused poetic form 'و + ز' (and from) linking two origins. آنک introduces a relative clause ('who/that'), استاد is the predicate noun 'a master', and بود is the past-tense copula 'was' (so: 'there was one who was a master').
segments.s654-g322: وز استاد خویشش هنر یاد بود: وز again functions as 'and from' (linking the clauses). استاد is 'the master' (the antecedent), خویشش ('خویش' with enclitic -ش) means 'his own' or 'of himself' modifying whose art, هنر is 'art/skill', and یاد بود ('یاد' + copula بود) here means 'was learned/remembered' — i.e. the art was known/taught by that master (past tense).
segments.s654-g323: ز ایران وز کشور نیمروز: ز = 'from'; وز = 'and from' joining two origins. ایران = 'Iran' (the land) and کشور نیمروز = 'the realm/land of Nimruz' (نیمروز here as the regional name). The phrase lists origins of people mentioned next.
segments.s654-g324: همه کارداران گیتی‌فروز: همه quantifies 'all/everyone'; کارداران is the plural noun 'managers/administrators/skillful men'; گیتی‌فروز is an epithet/adjective after the noun meaning 'world‑illuminating' or 'illustrious', modifying those administrators.
segments.s654-g325: همه گرد کرد اندران شارستان: همه 'all/everyone' is the object, گرد کرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'gathered' (کرد with گرد — classical compound), اندران = 'in/into it' (in that place), and شارستان = 'the city'. Word order follows classical Persian (object + verb + locative).
segments.s654-g326: که هم شارستان بود و هم کارستان: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/for that', هم ... هم ... is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...', شارستان 'a city', بود past copula 'was', و 'and', کارستان here means 'a place of feats/marvels' — so the clause says the place was both an ordinary city and a wonder-working place.
segments.s654-g327: اسیران که از بربر آورده بود: اسیران 'the prisoners' is the head noun; که introduces the relative clause 'that/whom'; از + بربر = 'from the barbarians'; آورده بود is the compound past (آورده + بود) meaning 'had brought' — here بود functions as the auxiliary forming past perfect.
segments.s654-g328: ز روم وز هر جای کازرده بود: ز روم = 'from Rome'; وز = 'and from' (linking); هر جای = 'every place/wherever'; کازرده is a proper name (a place-name here) modifying جای; بود is the past copula 'were/was' completing the statement about the prisoners' origins.
segments.s654-g177: This noun phrase lists three coordinated items as the subject: 'دبیران' (secretaries) + 'زروان' (a proper/name or personified Time) + 'دستور شاه' (literally “the king's دستور”). The conjunction 'و' simply links the items; the final noun 'شاه' is in apposition to 'دستور' forming ‘dastūr-e shāh’ (the king’s دستور). In classical Persian such a compound can serve as a plural subject for the following verb.
segments.s654-g178: 'برفتند' is the past-tense, third-person plural verb 'they departed/they went'. 'یک روز' is a temporal adverbial 'one day' (here 'once' / 'one day' = at one time). 'پویان' is an adjectival/participial word meaning 'on the move / seeking' describing the travelers or their activity. 'به راه' is the prepositional phrase 'on the road/into the way' (به + راه). Word order places the verb first (common in poetry) with time and manner following.
segments.s654-g179: 'سخن رفت' is an impersonal/idiomatic expression meaning 'there was talk' or 'speech went (on)'; 'رفت' here functions like 'was said' or 'proceeded'. 'چندی' is an adverbial meaning 'for a while'. The preposition 'ز' (poetic variant of از) introduces the topic 'افسون و بند' — 'about charms/enchantments and bonds/fetters'. Note the verb-subject/topic ordering is flexible in poetry.
segments.s654-g180: This clause continues the consequence/topic: 'ز جادوی و آهرمن پرگزند' — 'they suffered/had harm from magic and Ahriman.' 'ز' = 'from' (poetic), 'جادوی' = 'of magic/sorcery' (genitive), 'آهرمن' names the evil spirit Ahriman, and 'پرگزند' is an adjective/predicative phrase meaning 'full of hurt / harmed' (literally 'full of harm'). The whole phrase attributes harm to those agents.
segments.s654-g181: 'به موبد' is the indirect object 'to the mobad (the Zoroastrian priest)'. 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); 'پس شهریار' places the speaker ('then the king') after the reported act — a poetic inversion where 'پس' = 'then' or 'thereupon' and 'شهریار' names the speaker. Overall: 'Thus the king said to the mobad.'
segments.s654-g182: This is the content of the king's speech. 'که' introduces the reported clause ('that' / 'that: ...'). 'دل را' is the direct object 'the heart' (marked with the accusative rā). 'به نیرنگ' = 'with trickery/deceit' (prepositional phrase). 'رنجه مدار' is an archaic/poetic negative command: 'رنجه' (be distressed) together with 'مدار' yields 'do not distress' — i.e., 'do not let the heart be troubled by deceit.' The imperative sense is expressed by this verbal construction.
segments.s654-g183: 'سخن جز به یزدان' = 'speech/words except (be) to/of God' — i.e., 'speak only of God' or 'let your talk be only about the divine.' 'جز' is the exception particle 'except'. 'و از دین مگوی' continues with a prohibitive: 'and do not speak of/bring up religion' — 'مگوی' is the negative imperative 'do not say/tell'. Together the line issues prohibitions about topics of speech.
segments.s654-g184: 'ز' = 'from' introduces source/origin. 'نیرنگ جادو' is a genitive compound 'the trickery of magic' (nīrang + jādū). 'شگفتی' is the object 'wonders/marvels', and 'مجوی' is the negative imperative 'do not seek.' The clause thus warns: 'Do not seek marvels from the deceptions of sorcery.'
segments.s654-g345: جز از is a limiting phrase meaning 'nothing except / apart from'; here جز + از introduces the sole thing he had. داد و آباد کردن جهان is a coordinated noun/infinitive phrase: داد (a noun 'justice') coordinated with آباد کردن ('to make prosperous'), with جهان as the object of آباد کردن. The whole segment functions as the sole content of his intent.
segments.s654-g346: نبودش is past negative with the clitic -ش referring to 'him' (i.e. 'he did not have it'); the clause says that in his heart (به دل) there was no other aim. آشکار و نهان is a fixed adverbial pair 'openly and secretly / outwardly and inwardly' modifying the mental state ('in his heart, both apparent and hidden').
segments.s654-g347: چو here = 'when'; زمانه is personified as Fate/fortune (subject). او را is the direct object clitic-marked; ز شاهی (from the kingship) indicates what he was removed from. ببرد is the 3rd-person present/past narrative form ('takes away' / 'removed'), used in classical narrative to express the action of 'time' taking him away.
segments.s654-g348: The clause has an inverted, poetic word order: the implied subject (زمانه) 'سپرد' (handed over) و همه تاج (the whole crown) is the thing handed, and 'دیگر کسی را' 'to another person' is the recipient, with را marking the (here indirect) object. سپرد is past-tense 'entrusted/handed over.'
segments.s654-g349: چنان دان is an imperative instruction 'consider it thus / know it so' (دان = consider/know). The subordinate clause introduced by که states the content: یک سر فریبست = 'one (single) thing/head is deception' with فریبست as the copular clause 'is deception'; و بس adds 'and that is enough.' The phrase یک سر is poetic for 'one single (thing/point)'.
segments.s654-g350: بلندی و پستی is the paired abstract nouns 'highness and lowness' (status extremes). نماند is past-tense negative 'does not remain / there remains none,' and بکس ('baks') means 'a person' — together: 'no person remains in high or low' (i.e. distinctions vanish). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: predicate (نماند) precedes the (indefinite) person.
segments.s654-g351: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. جنگ خاقان و هیتال is the object 'the war of the Khagan and Hital.' گیر at the end is an imperative 'take / seize' (poetic placement of the verb after its object is normal in Ferdowsi). The whole segment is a command to undertake that war.
segments.s654-g352: چو رزم آیدت پیش is a temporal subordinate clause 'when battle comes before you' (آیدت = 'it comes to you/affects you'); پیش = 'before / in front of.' The final کوپال گیر is another imperative 'seize/take Koopal' with Koopal as the object (here likely a name). The two imperatives are consecutive commands linked by the temporal clause.
segments.s654-g145: به ایوان مهبود در کس نماند is built with a locative prepositional phrase up front. به (here: toward/into/at) + ایوان مهبود marks location 'at Mahbud's iwan'; در کس نماند is an existential negative: در 'inside' + کس 'anyone/no one' + نماند 'remained' (past), so the clause means 'no one remained inside.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect, but the verb نماند is the main verb governing the negation.
segments.s654-g146: ز خویشان او درجهان بس نماند is another negative-existential clause. ز 'from/of' marks the class (his relatives); خویشان او is the genitive phrase 'his relatives'. بس نماند 'no enough/none remained' is an idiomatic negation meaning 'not a single one remained.' The element درجهان here is elliptical/compound (classically read as در جهان 'in the world' or a fixed expression); the verb نماند again carries past negation.
segments.s654-g147: به تاراج داد is a light-verb construction where به تاراج 'to pillage/for pillage' is the prepositional/goal phrase and داد (past of دادن) is the main verb 'gave, delivered.' آن همه خواسته is the object: آن همه 'all that' + خواسته (nominalized past participle) 'that was wanted/desired' — together 'all that was to be taken.'
segments.s654-g148: زن و کودک و گنج آراسته is a simple coordinate list of the things given to pillage. The nouns (زن, کودک, گنج) are placed before the adjective آراسته 'adorned,' which here qualifies گنج (treasure) but by position and poetry may color the whole list; the conjunction و links the items in classical Persian list order.
segments.s654-g149: رسیده ... به کام is a perfect/result state: رسیده (past participle/predicative) 'has arrived' or 'has been reached' + به کام 'to fulfilment/satisfaction.' از آن کار gives the cause/source 'from that deed,' and زروان (Zurvān) is the personified Time/agent whose 'hour' or influence is described as having come to fulfilment. Word order places the resulting state (رسیده) first for emphasis.
segments.s654-g150: گهی کام دید اندر آن گاه نام contains two short clauses collapsed for poetic brevity. گهی 'now and then/sometimes' modifies the verb دید 'saw' with کام as its object: 'sometimes saw satisfaction/desire fulfilled.' اندر آن گاه 'in that moment' introduces a temporal phrase; نام 'name' (i.e. fame) stands nominative with an elided verb ('appeared' or 'was'), a common poetic omission — so 'at that moment came a name/fame.'
segments.s654-g151: به نزدیک او شد is a light-clause meaning 'approached him' (به + noun indicating direction/goal + شد 'became/came' used as past 'came/approached'). جهود ارجمند follows as the subject in inverted order: جهود (here the archaic ethnic/occupational term 'Jew') + ارجمند 'esteemed/noble' — 'a noble Jew came near him.'
segments.s654-g152: برافراخت سر تا بابر بلند is a pictorial past action: برافراخت (past of برافراشتن) 'raised/hoisted up' + سر 'head' as the direct object; تا introduces the extent/comparison 'up to/as far as,' and بابر بلند is a comparative reference (a proper name or epithet plus بلند 'high/lofty'). The clause therefore means he 'raised his head up high, even up to Babur (i.e. to a lofty height),' with the final verb elided as stylistic compression.
segments.s654-g257: همه گنج (object) precedes the agent زروان in poetic word order; بدیشان is the dative clitic phrase 'to them' placed before the verb. نمود is the past tense of نمودن/نشان دادن 'to show' and here is a finite past ('showed'). The whole clause reads literally 'showed all the treasure to them (Zurvān)'.
segments.s654-g258: دگر here means 'besides/in addition'; هرچ آن = 'whatever that (there was)' functions as an indefinite object; داشت is the simple past 'had'; مرد جهود is the subject 'the Jew-man' placed after the verb in this inverted poetic order. Literally: 'besides, whatever that Jew had.'
segments.s654-g259: روانش = روان + enclitic possessive -ش 'his soul'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by'; مهبود is an epithet 'the moon‑like one' (the beloved); بریان is a metaphorical adjective 'burnt/consumed (by love)'; شدی is a past-form auxiliary meaning 'became/was made' — together: 'his soul was consumed by the moon‑like one.'
segments.s654-g260: شب تیره تا روز = a temporal phrase 'night dark until day', where تا marks the span 'from ... to / through'. گریان is an adjective 'weeping' describing روز; بدی here is an archaic/poetic past copula sense roughly 'was / he passed' (the verse implies he endured dark nights and tearful days). The clause therefore describes the suffering endured night and day.
segments.s654-g261: ز یزدان = 'to/from God' (a prepositional phrase indicating the addressee of the plea). همی‌خواستی is the imperfect/continuous past with the old prefix همی- meaning 'kept beseeching/was imploring'. زینهار is an interjection 'take heed / have mercy' used as the content of the plea.
segments.s654-g262: همی‌ریختی is the continuous/iterative past 'you/he was pouring / kept pouring' (homogeneous with همی‑ prefix); خون دل 'blood of the heart' is a poetic metaphor for deep grief; برکنار here means 'aside / out (poured out apart)', indicating the pouring away of inner sorrow. The clause describes his shedding the blood of his heart (i.e., pouring out his grief).
segments.s654-g263: به درویش marks the indirect object 'to the dervish/poor man'. بخشید is the past of بخشیدن and here means 'gave/bestowed' (not 'forgave'). بسیار چیز = 'many things' (direct object placed after the verb in this poetic order).
segments.s654-g264: زبانی = 'a tongue / speech' (i.e., style of speaking). پر از آفرین = 'full of praise' (پر + از expresses 'full of'); داشت is the past 'had' showing possession; نیز adds 'also/too'. The clause means he likewise possessed a tongue full of praise (he praised or spoke praises).
segments.s654-g201: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that'; مهبود is a descriptive noun 'the moon‑like one' serving as the clause subject; بر دست ما is an agent/instrument phrase ('at/by our hand(s)') where بر + دست indicates 'by the hand of'; کشته شد is a passive past construction 'was killed', with شد functioning as the auxiliary/passive marker.
segments.s654-g202: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' linking result; دوده را uses را as a topical/definite marker pointing to 'smoke' as what is affected; روز is the subject 'the day'; برگشته شد is an inchoative/passive sequence 'was turned/returned' (i.e. 'became turned'), with شد here indicating becoming.
segments.s654-g203: مگر here has a rhetorical/optative force ('would that' / 'if only'); کردگار is the subject 'the Creator/God'; آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/clearly'; کند is the present/subjunctive form 'may (he) do' (jussive/optative sense), expressing a wish.
segments.s654-g204: دل و مغز ما را is the definite object phrase 'our heart and (our) mind/brain' with را marking it as the object; مدارا کند is a verb phrase 'may (he) show gentleness/tolerance' where کند is again the optative/subjunctive 'may do' and مدارا is the abstract noun 'gentleness/tolerance' functioning as the verbal object/complement.
segments.s654-g205: که introduces the clause; آلوده is an adjective 'tainted/soiled' describing the following noun; بینم is first‑person present 'I see' (here with continuative همی giving an ongoing sense); ز (here contracted as 'زو') expresses 'from/of him/it' linking the cause/source; سخن is the direct object 'speech/words'.
segments.s654-g206: پر از means 'full of'; دردم is the possessive form 'my pain' (درد + -م); the following از روزگار کهن gives the source/ground 'from the ancient times' (روزگار کهن = 'ancient days/times').
segments.s654-g207: همی‌رفت is the imperfect/continuous past 'was going' (همی marking habitual/continuous aspect); با introduces the accompanying circumstance 'with'; دل پر از درد وغم is a noun phrase 'a heart full of pain and sorrow' (پر از = 'full of', وغم = 'and grief/sorrow' attached to درد).
segments.s654-g208: This is a pair of nominal descriptions: پرآژنگ is an adjective 'ornamented/ornate' modifying رخ 'face/visage'; دیدگان 'the eyes' is a separate noun; پر ز نم is 'full of moisture/tears' (پر = 'full', ز = poetic 'from/of', نم = 'tear/moisture'), giving a vivid image of an adorned face and tearful eyes.
segments.s654-g265: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'); یزدان is the subject 'God'; گناهش is 'his sin' (گناه + possessive -ش). ببخشد is a present-subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may forgive' or 'forgives' in a potential sense. مگر here means 'except'/'unless' and sets up the exception continued in the next segment.
segments.s654-g266: ستمگر ('the oppressor') is the actor of this clause; نخواند is the negative third-person verb 'does not call' (here meaning 'refuses/does not call'); ورا is the object pronoun 'him/to him'; دادگر is a predicative label 'just' (i.e. 'call him "just"'). Word order reflects poetic inversion but reads: 'the oppressor does not call him just.'
segments.s654-g267: کسی 'someone' begins the clause; کو here functions as a relative particle ('who/that') linking to بود 'was/is' (past/form of 'to be'), so 'one who was/is'; پاک و یزدان‌پرست are descriptive adjectives 'pure and pious'. The clause describes the kind of person being spoken of.
segments.s654-g268: نیازد is from نیازیدن used here in the sense 'needs' (or 'would need'); به + کردار بد is a prepositional phrase 'to/for evil deed(s)'; هیچ دست literally 'no hand' — دست functions as 'help/assistance' here, so the phrase means 'needs no hand (help) in evil deeds.' The negative notion is carried by 'هیچ' after the verb.
segments.s654-g269: که + گرچند introduces a concessive clause ('that/although' — گرچند = 'although'). بد کردن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'doing evil' (کردن as light verb), آسان بود is the predicate 'was easy' (بود as past tense here). The clause concedes ease of wrongdoing.
segments.s654-g270: به فرجام is a locative/prepositional phrase 'at/to the outcome'; ز + و together represent 'from/of it' (here separated in the tokenization), pointing back to the action's consequence. جان is the subject 'the life/soul' and هراسان بود is the predicate 'was frightened' (هراسان + بود). The whole means 'at the end of it his soul was fearful.'
segments.s654-g271: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بد modifies دل ('a bad/ill heart' or 'evil-hearted'); سنگ خارا is a compound-like phrase 'stone (سنگ) flint (خارا)' meaning 'as hard as flint/stone'. شود is the subjunctive/potential 'becomes' — together: 'if the heart becomes like flint.'
segments.s654-g272: نماند is the negative third-person form 'does not remain' (here 'nothing remains'); نهان 'hidden' follows the verb as the thing that does not remain hidden. آشکارا شود is a parallel clause 'it becomes manifest/openly revealed' (آشکارا = 'openly', شود = 'becomes'). The two halves state cause and consequence: once the heart is hardened, nothing stays hidden and things are revealed.
segments.s654-g169: نداند is a classical third-person negative verb form meaning “knows not / does not know.” جز از introduces an exception and combines with کردگار جهان (“the Maker/Creator of the world”) as the excluded/only-known source; word order (verb before the prepositional exception) is typical of Ferdowsi’s elevated style.
segments.s654-g170: ازان (“from that / from Him”) refers back to the Creator. آشکارا and نهان both qualify درستی (rightness/truth): آشکارا before and نهان after the noun form an emphatic pair (“manifest and hidden [truth]”); the copula is omitted as usual, so the phrase states that truth, both open and hidden, proceeds from Him.
segments.s654-g171: وزان = و + ازان, “and from that,” linking this movement to the preceding fact. جایگه is the noun “place,” followed by the preposition سوی “toward,” and نخچیرگاه is the goal “hunting-ground”; the phrase is a simple prepositional direction phrase describing motion.
segments.s654-g172: بیامد is the past (3rd sg.) verb “came.” چنان is an intensifier “such/so,” modifying the following nominal phrase داغِ دل (“burning/aching of the heart” = heartache). کینه‌خواه is a compound: کینه (grudge/hatred) + خواه (‘seeking, desirous of’) functioning adjectivally/participially = “seeking vengeance” or “vengeful.” Together they describe his manner of coming.
segments.s654-g173: ز هر کس = “from every/each person.” خواستی is the past tense (“you sought/wanted”) and takes the following phrase بره برسخن as its object. بره here is used figuratively/metaphorically (literally ‘lamb’) to mean a gentle/soft token or a gentle word; برسخن (“for/on speech”) marks that this is sought in the realm of talk—i.e. you sought soft/agreeable words from everyone.
segments.s654-g174: ز گفتارها = “from the speeches/utterances.” دل is the thing affected (“the heart”) and بیاراستی is a past tense verb (second-person in this construction) meaning roughly “you adorned/arrayed/settled” (i.e. you set your heart aright or armed/beautified it) with those sayings. The construction shows instrument/source: you took comfort or styling of heart from others’ words.
segments.s654-g175: سراینده is the subject “the poet/songster.” بسیار modifies همراه and indicates quantity (“many” companions). کرد is the past tense verb “did/made/took along”: together همراه کرد = “took companions” or “gathered attendants.” The clause structure is Subject + (quantity + object) + verb.
segments.s654-g176: به افسانه‌ها is a prepositional phrase “to/into the legends/tales.” راه کوتاه کرد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: کوتاه qualifies راه (“path/way”) and کرد is the verb “made/caused,” so literally “he made the way short,” i.e. he briefly or succinctly turned the narrative toward the tales (took a short route into the legends).
segments.s654-g129: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” بشنید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb “he heard.” ز (poetic) marks the source/object of hearing (“of/from”), and شاه نوشین‌روان is the noun phrase ‘the king Nushin‑rōvān’ (title + epithet) acting as the subject about whom the preceding verb is said.
segments.s654-g130: نگه کرد is a common periphrastic verb: نگه (a noun ‘gaze/look’) + کرد (past of کردن) = ‘he cast/looked.’ روشن functions adverbially/attributively describing the gaze as ‘clear/bright.’ به هر دوجوان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/upon each of the two youths,’ where هر (each) precedes the noun (دوجوان).
segments.s654-g131: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for'/'that'). خوالیگرش is an archaic/obscure lexical item (here left unspecified); مام ایشان is ‘their mam’ (mam = wet‑nurse/mother in dialectal/older usage) where ایشان is the 3rd‑person plural possessive reference. بدی here is an archaic form functioning as a past action (see note below) and links the nurse’s action to the following phrase.
