segments.s654-g132: This continues the subordinate clause: خردمند is an adjective ‘wise’ describing the beneficiaries, linked by و (‘and’) to the following phrase. با کام ایشان is a prepositional phrase ‘with/for their desire(s)’ (کام = desire/satisfaction), and the final بدی is the same archaic past verbal form completing the sense ‘had given/caused’—so the whole segment says the nurse made them wise and fulfilled their wishes.
segments.s654-g133: جوانان is the plural noun ‘the youths/young men’ (subject). ز here means ‘from/by/due to’ marking cause or source (‘by purity’). پاکی is ‘purity/chastity’; وز in this line functions as a connective (an older/poetic variant) linking to راستی (‘truth/honesty’), so the phrase names the qualities associated with the youths.
segments.s654-g134: نوشتند is past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural ‘they wrote/inscribed.’ بر پشت دست is a locative phrase ‘on the back of the hand’ (بر = on/upon, پشت = back, دست = hand). آستی is an adverbial/qualifying word here meaning ‘gently/slowly’ that modifies نوشتند.
segments.s654-g135: همان چون is a temporal formula meaning ‘just as / no sooner than’; همان = ‘just/exactly,’ and چون functions as ‘as/when.’ بخوردند here is past tense plural and in this context means ‘they drank/partook (of).’ از کاسه شیر is the prepositional phrase ‘from the bowl of milk’ (از = from, کاسه = bowl, شیر = milk).
segments.s654-g136: توگویی is an idiomatic phrase ‘as if’/‘it was as though’ (here the lexical entry in the list doesn’t reflect that idiomatic use). بخستند is an older/poetic past form meaning roughly ‘were struck/affected’ in context; it combines with به تیر (‘by an arrow’) where به marks the agent/instrument and هر دو specifies ‘both.’ The clause therefore describes an immediate, sudden misfortune affecting both youths.
segments.s654-g209: به منزل رسید is a prepositional phrase + verb meaning 'arrived at the dwelling' (به + object + past verb). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time/then' placed before the subject. شهریار is the subject (king). The verb رسید is past‑tense, 3rd person singular; Persian word order here places the verb before the subject for stylistic/poetic effect.
segments.s654-g210: سراپرده is the noun 'inner chamber / one behind the curtain' and functions here as the agent/subject. زد is the past 3rd singular of زدن used as 'knocked/struck' (here intransitive/performative: someone knocked). بر لب جویبار is a locative prepositional phrase (بر = on/upon + لب = brink/edge + جویبار = stream) indicating where the knocking occurred.
segments.s654-g211: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. زروان is the proper name Zurvān (the subject). بیامد is the past tense 'came'. به + پرده سرای is a prepositional phrase 'into the curtained hall/chamber' (به = to/into). Note Persian allows verb before subject in poetic register.
segments.s654-g212: ز (poetic) indicates the beneficiary/related party ('with / for / from' the stranger) and is followed by بیگانه 'the stranger'. پردخت is a noun/verb meaning 'adornment/embellishment' (here the idea 'they adorned/prepared'); کردند is the past 3rd plural 'they did / they made', so پردخت کردند جای = 'they made/arrayed a place'. جای is the direct object 'place'.
segments.s654-g213: ز جادو سخن رفت is an idiomatic phrasing where ز = 'about/of', جادو = 'magic', سخن = 'speech/subject', and رفت (literally 'went') means the subject 'turned to' or 'the conversation moved to' magic. وز here is a poetic conjunction (variant of و) linking the next phrase. شهد و شیر are literal 'honey and milk' — metaphors for sweet speech.
segments.s654-g214: بدو = 'to him' (dative). گفت شد is a passive/periphrastic expression: گفت (root 'say' / 'speech') plus شد ('became' / auxiliary) yields 'was said' / 'was told'. این سخن دلپذیر is the patient/theme 'this pleasant speech' (این = this, سخن = speech, دلپذیر = pleasant). Together: 'this pleasant speech was said to him.'
segments.s654-g215: ز indicates 'from/of' (he asked of the person named). مهبود is a descriptive name 'mahabud' (moon‑beloved / moon‑like one). زان پس is a temporal phrase 'thereafter/after that' (زان = from that, پس = then/after). بپرسید is past 'asked' (3rd singular past), and شاه is the subject 'the king' (placed at the end for emphasis/poetic order).
segments.s654-g216: ز فرزند او = 'concerning/from his son' (ز = about/from; فرزند = son/child; او = his). تا introduces an indirect question/clause here ('that / so as to / introducing the question'); چرا = 'why' (interrogative); شد is past 'became'; تباه is the adjective 'ruined/corrupt'. The whole segment is an indirect question: 'about his son, (he asked) why he became ruined.'
segments.s654-g305: نشست is the past‑tense verb 'sat' (classical Persian perfect/past) with the subject omitted (third person). اندر is the preposition 'in/within' placed before the locative noun phrase ایوان گوهرنگار ('the jewel‑adorned iwan'), so the clause reads literally 'sat in the jewel‑adorned iwan.'
segments.s654-g306: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle here that intensifies or frames the action (continuous or habitual narrative flavor). رای زد is a light‑verb construction: رای ('counsel/plan') plus زد (zadan 'to strike/make') meaning 'took counsel' or 'made a plan/decision.' با plus می‌ومیگسار ('with wine and the cupbearer') is an instrumental/companion phrase describing the setting in which the counsel was taken.
segments.s654-g307: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an unspecified city). شارستان 'city' is the object; کرد is the verb 'made/built' (past). به آیین روم literally 'in the manner/rite of Rome' uses به to mark manner ('in the Roman fashion'). The whole clause means 'he built/established a city in the Roman fashion.'
segments.s654-g308: فزون از 'more than' introduces a comparative quantity; here it governs the measure دو فرسنگ ('two parasangs'). بالای بوم is a locative phrase 'above/upon the land' (بوم = homeland/ground). Word order places the quantity phrase before the locative qualifier: 'more than two parasangs above the land.'
segments.s654-g309: بدو is a deictic/directional particle 'to it/unto that' linking this description to the previously mentioned city. اندرون ('inside/within') introduces a list of internal features: کاخ (palace), ایوان (iwan/porch) and باغ (garden). The conjunctions و simply join the nouns in a straightforward enumerative structure.
segments.s654-g310: به یک دست is an idiomatic locative expression meaning 'on one side' (دست used as 'side'). رود is the noun 'river' placed after that phrase; the second half repeats the pattern (و به یک دست راغ) to contrast the opposite side. The clause omits a copular verb (a common poetic compression), so the effect is 'on one side a river, on the other side a rāgh.'
segments.s654-g311: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' setting up the action. بد here is an archaic narrative particle (classical usage) meaning roughly 'thereupon/then' rather than its literal 'bad' sense. بروم is a narrative motion verb ('went/entered'); in classical Persian the form can carry a historical/narrative sense equivalent to past 'he went.' اندرون پادشهر is the locative 'into the inner capital/royal city.'
segments.s654-g312: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that.' کسری is the proper name 'Khosrow/Kisra.' بپیمود is past of پیمودن 'he traversed/spanned' (here 'had traversed/visited'); برداشت is the transitive past 'he took/lifted' used idiomatically as 'seized/claimed,' and بهر (for/for the purpose of) marks the relation 'for himself' or 'as his own.' Together: 'which Khosrow had traversed and taken for himself.'
segments.s654-g193: ز + NP + یاد کردن is a classical construction meaning 'to remember (someone)'. Here ز marks the person remembered (object of یاد کردن). یاد (a noun) combines with کرد to form the verbal idea 'remembered'. Word order is topical: 'ز مهبود و هر دو پسر یاد کرد' = 'he remembered Mahbud and both sons.'
segments.s654-g194: برآورد is a past narrative verb meaning 'raised/brought up' or 'came up'. The phrase بر لب 'upon (his) lip' gives the locus, یکی functions as an indefinite 'a', and باد سرد = 'a cold wind/breeze'. Literally 'he raised (or there rose) upon the lip a cold wind' = 'a cold breeze came to his lips.'
segments.s654-g195: به + proper name + نگه کرد = 'he cast his gaze to/toward Zurvān' (نگه here = 'gaze, look' and کرد makes the verb). و links the next clause; خامش بماند = 'he kept/stayed silent' (خامش = silent/silence, بماند used here narratively = 'remained').
segments.s654-g196: سبک باره modifies گامزن: a compound sense 'light-burdened' or 'light-laden' marcher/walker (i.e. a traveler with little baggage). را is the definite object marker placed before the verb; براند (راند) = 'drove away/drove off.' Word order is object (با را) before verb, a common classical Persian order.
segments.s654-g197: روانش = 'his soul/spirit' (possessed by -ش). ز اندیشه = 'from (or because of) thought'; پر دود بود = 'was full of smoke' (پر = 'full (of)' modifying دود). Together: 'his spirit was full of the smoke of thought' = 'his mind was clouded by thought.'
segments.s654-g198: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). The structure here is 'X بداندیش Y بود' = 'X was ill‑disposed toward Y.' So زروان is the subject, بداندیش an adjectival predicate meaning 'malicious/ill‑thinking toward', and مهبود is the target of that ill will; بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s654-g199: همی‌گفت is an iterative/past narrative 'he kept saying' or 'he would say'. کین here functions as an archaic demonstrative 'this' (common in classical Persian: 'کین/کاین مرد' = 'this man'), modifying مرد ناسازگار = 'an ill‑matched/discordant man' (i.e. hostile or unsuited).
segments.s654-g200: ندانم = 'I do not know' (negated present of دانستن used narratively = 'I don't know' / 'I cannot say'). چه کرد = 'what he did'. اندران روزگار = 'in that time/among those days' (اندران = 'in/among it/therein' + روزگار = 'time/fate/that age').
segments.s654-g241: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase 'once again / anew' (دگر = again, باره = instance) placed before the verb; کرد is the perfective past 'did/uttered' used here as the main verb. آن سخن is the direct object 'that speech/those words', and خواستار is a noun 'the petitioner' identifying who spoke; classical Persian often puts the verb before its subject and object.
segments.s654-g242: به پیش introduces a locative phrase 'in the presence of / before'; ردان here names the group ('those who drive/horsemen' in context) and is followed by a genitive chain دادگر شهریار, where دادگر functions as an epithet 'the just' modifying شهریار 'the king'. The whole phrase specifies the audience/location of the speaking.
segments.s654-g243: بفرمود پس is a polite/past reportive verb 'then he ordered/commanded'; تا introduces the purpose or the content of the order ('that / so that') and governs the following noun phrase دو دار بلند 'two tall poles' (دار here = pole/gallows, بلند = tall). Word order places the verb before the purpose phrase.
segments.s654-g244: فروهشته is a perfective verb 'let down / cast down / hung' describing what was done; از دار پیچان کمند is a prepositional phrase 'from the twisting/winding poles (دار پیچان) [the] snares (کمند)'. پیچان is an adjective modifying دار, and کمند is the object produced/let down from them.
segments.s654-g245: بزد is the past simple transitive 'struck / smote'. مرد دژخیم functions as a nominal group 'the brutal/torturing man' (دژخیم qualifying مرد) and پیش درش is a locative phrase 'before his gate' (در = gate/door, ش = his). Classical Persian often omits an explicit object pronoun; context supplies who was struck.
segments.s654-g246: نظاره بروبر is an idiomatic nominal phrase 'a spectacle/view for display' (بروبر = face-to-face / openly, here 'for all to see'); همه کشورش is the genitive phrase 'his whole country/realm'. The copula is omitted: the clause means 'the sight was before his entire realm' (i.e., everyone in his realm saw it).
segments.s654-g247: به یک دار introduces location 'on one gallows/pole' (دار = gallows/pole). زروان is a proper name (Zurvān) placed after the location to indicate who was placed there; و دیگر 'and the other' introduces the second item, and جهود here (archaic) refers to 'the Jews' or 'a Jew' as part of a compound; in this construction efforts or peoples can be named after the place they are put. The phrase lists two things placed on the poles.
segments.s654-g248: کشنده here functions as 'killer' or an epithet 'the Jew-killer' linked to the previous noun; برآهخت is the perfective 'he raised / hoisted up' (i.e., hanged/put up). و تندی نمود is a final clause 'and he showed fierceness' (تندی = fierceness/harshness, نمود = showed/did), with نمود as a light verb marking resultive display of temperament.
segments.s654-g1: چنین گفت موبد که introduces reported speech: 'چنین' (thus) + 'گفت' (said) + 'موبد' (the mobed/priest) and the complementizer 'که' ('that') begins the subordinate clause. 'بر تخت عاج' is a prepositional phrase (بر = on/upon) with 'تخت' (throne) modified by 'عاج' (ivory). The clause sets location/background for what follows.
segments.s654-g2: This clause completes the subordinate clause begun by 'که'. 'چو' here means 'like/as' (making a comparison with Khosrow), 'کسری' is the proper name Khosrow, and 'کسی نیز ننهاد تاج' is a negative statement: 'no one (kasi) also/else (نیز) placed/set (ننهاد, past negative of نهاد) a crown (تاج)'. Word order is typical Persian: topic/phrase first, verb at the end.
segments.s654-g3: This is a series of prepositional phrases describing spheres or modes of conduct: 'به بزم' (in/at the feast), 'و برزم' (and on the battlefield — an archaic/poetic pairing with بزم), and 'و به پرهیز' (and in abstinence). 'وداد' here functions as an abstract noun meaning 'gave love/kindness' or as the quality being referred to; syntactically the whole phrase supplies the attribute that the following clause will judge.
segments.s654-g4: This is the judgement clause: 'چنو' ('such'/'like this') + 'کس' (person/one) + 'ندارد' (does not exist/there is not) + 'ز شاهان' (among the kings; 'ز' = from/of) + 'به یاد' (in memory/remembrance). Together: 'There is no such person among kings in memory.' Note Persian SOV order with the verb 'ندارد' near the middle of the clause here.
segments.s654-g5: 'ز دانندگان' is a source phrase: 'ز' = from, 'دانندگان' = the knowledgeable/experts. 'دانش آموختی' is a past tense verb phrase (آموختی = you learned/you acquired); word order places the source first, then the object ('دانش' = knowledge) and the verb final, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s654-g6: 'دلش را' is the definite object (دل = heart, ش = his), marked by the object marker 'را'. 'بدانش' is a contracted/prepositional form meaning 'by/with that knowledge' (بـ + دانش). The verb 'برافروختی' is second-person past 'you kindled/you set afire' — here metaphorical: you inflamed his heart (with knowledge).
segments.s654-g7: 'خور وخواب' is a paired nominal phrase meaning 'eating and sleeping' (خور here denotes eating/activity related to food), 'با موبدان' is a prepositional phrase 'with the mobeds (priests)', and 'داشتی' is the second-person past/habitual 'you had/you used to have' — together: 'you ate and slept with the mobeds.'
segments.s654-g8: همی is an adverb meaning 'always/ever'; 'سر به دانش' is an idiomatic phrase 'head to knowledge' (i.e., attentiveness to learning), and 'برافراشتی' is second-person past 'you lifted/you raised' (literally 'you raised your head toward knowledge'). The clause says you continually turned your attention to learning. Word order: adverb — object/phrase — verb final.
segments.s654-g57: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The line lists sources to be avoided: نیرنگ (a noun) is the first item 'trickery'. وز here is a fused poetic sequence equivalent to 'و ز' (and from), joining the next items; this is common in epic diction. تنبل and جادویی are two coordinated adjectives/nouns 'laziness' and 'sorcery/magic' joined by و ('and').
segments.s654-g58: Again ز = 'from' introduces another group. کردار کژی is a noun + noun (or noun + adjective) 'crooked conduct / crooked deed(s)'. وز (as above) links the next item (acting as fused 'و ز'). بدخویی is the final noun 'ill-temper / ill-nature'. The whole segment continues the catalogue introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s654-g59: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. زروان is the proper name Zurvān (here the deity/personification of Time). به + گفتار 'به گفتار' marks relation to 'speech/words' ('at/on the speech' or 'regarding the words'). مرد جهود is a noun phrase 'the man/jew (مرد + جهود — 'the Jewish man') using an archaic plural/collective noun for Jews as an identifier.
segments.s654-g60: نگه کرد is a two-word verbal sequence: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (past auxiliary 'did') — together 'he turned/held his gaze' (past). وزان 'and from that / thereupon' links the consequence. سان 'so/thus' modifies سخنها (the plural 'words/speeches'), and شنود is an archaic 3rd-person present/epic-form of 'hear' ('he hears' / 'he listened'), used here in narrative to mean he heard those words as a result.
segments.s654-g61: برو is the imperative 'go'. راز بگشاد is an imperative infinitive construction where بگشاد (from گشودن) functions as 'open/reveal' (here used as a command). The conjunction و links to گفت here introducing reported speech; in the poem the verb 'said' is implied before the quoted instruction. این سخن 'this speech' is the direct object of the following negative imperative.
segments.s654-g62: به جز = 'except'. پیش جان literally 'in the presence of / before the soul' — پیش = 'before / in the presence of', جان = 'soul / beloved' (a term often used to mean the intimate/addressee). آشکارا مکن is the negative imperative: آشکارا 'openly' + مکن 'do not (do)'; together 'do not make (it) manifest/openly'. The whole phrase is the content of the injunction introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s654-g63: یکی چاره is 'one/ a certain remedy' (an indefinite/one solution). باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary'. تو را is the object/beneficiary clitic phrase 'for you' (in older/poetic word order the pronoun+را can precede the infinitive). ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/devise'; together the clause expresses obligation: 'You must make/devise a remedy' (or more literally, 'one remedy it is necessary to make for you').
segments.s654-g64: This is an infinitival adjunct explaining the remedy: زمانه here names 'Time / the fortunes of the age' (the thing to be dealt with). ز introduces the agent/source 'by/from/with' (poetic usage), مهبود is a compound-name 'the moon‑like one' or deity/cherished one, and پرداختن is the infinitive 'to pay / to render / to discharge' (here plausibly 'to pay tribute / to appease'). Grammatically the whole phrase is an infinitive clause (پرداختن ...) that specifies the action required as the remedy.
segments.s654-g121: پس is a temporal adverb 'then/thereupon'. خوان here is the noun 'feast, banquet' (subject). همی‌رفت is an archaic imperfective past 'was going/on-going' (classical mi-/hami- prefix). زروان is the personified 'time' (classical figure) rather than the proper-name deity reading; چو ... گرد = 'like/as ... round' (چو = 'as/like', گرد = 'around/round') describing how time moves.
segments.s654-g122: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech. گفت is the verb 'said' (the listed sense set gives lexical senses for conversation; treat it here as the speech verb). با before شاه indicates direction/recipient ('to' or 'with') and شاه 'king' is addressed; آزادمرد is an epithet/apposition 'noble man' (the speaker or the addressee's epithet depending on context).
segments.s654-g123: که‌ای is a classical vocative/particleal address ('O ...') built from که + ای; here it prefaces direct address. شاه 'king' followed by epithets نیک اختر ('good/auspicious star') and دادگر ('just one/just ruler') — adjectives and titles placed after or alongside the noun as honorifics.
segments.s654-g124: تو is the emphatic 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. بی‌چاشنی is a negative compound 'without seasoning' (بی- prefix + چاشنی 'seasoning'). دست خوردن is an infinitival/verb phrase 'to eat (with the) hand' (دست = 'hand', خوردن = 'to eat'); مبر is the classical prohibitive/negative imperative 'do not take/do not do'. The order places the pronoun and adverbial before the verb phrase, with مبر negating the action.
segments.s654-g125: که here functions as a connective 'for/because' introducing explanation. روی فلک = 'the face/surface of the sky' (روی = 'face/surface', فلک = 'heaven/firmament'). بخت خندان is a noun phrase 'smiling Fortune' (بخت = 'fortune', خندان = 'smiling'); تست is the archaic verbal copula 'is/it is yours' in classical Persian (here functioning as present/copular), but the provided sense list does not include this copular sense, so it is marked separately below.
segments.s654-g126: جهان 'the world' is the subject; روشن 'bright/illuminated' is a predicate adjective. از here has the causal/use sense 'because of' (classical 'from/because of'). تخت 'throne' and میدان 'field/arena' are the instruments/causes; تست again is the archaic copula ('is' / 'belongs to you' in context) not represented in the given senses.
segments.s654-g127: خورشگر 'the cook' is the agent. بیامیخت is a classical past tense verb 'mingled/combined' (بی- prefix with آمخت, classical formation). با is the preposition 'with' or 'into' here linking what is mixed: شیر 'milk' (not 'lion' in this context) and زهر 'poison' — the cook mixed poison into the milk.
segments.s654-g128: بداندیش را: بداندیش 'ill‑minded/ill‑wisher' is the target and را is the definite/object marker marking him as the affected person. باد here is the optative/exhortative particle 'may' (classical wish: 'may it be...'). زین in classical Persian functions as 'this' / 'of this' (an archaic demonstrative) but that sense was not supplied in the list, so marked -1. زهر 'poison' is the thing wished; بهر is the prepositional 'for/toward' completing the phrase — literally 'may this poison be for the ill‑wisher' (i.e., befall him).
segments.s654-g313: برآورد is a past-tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning 'raised/erected' or 'caused to be made.' زو here is the fused poetic dative 'از + او' meaning 'for him' (or 'for that one'). The phrase follows SOV tendencies of classical Persian: verb + indirect object + noun phrase, so the whole segment reads 'he erected for him tall palaces.'
segments.s654-g314: This clause contains an omitted copula: نبد (نبود) is the archaic negative copula 'was not / there was not,' followed by نزد کس ('in the view of anyone') and then درجهان ناپسند. Together they form a passive-style evaluation: 'was not, in anyone's sight in the world, disliked' — i.e. 'no one in the world found (them) unpleasant.' ناپسند is the predicate adjective 'disagreeable/unacceptable.'
segments.s654-g315: یکی is the indefinite 'one / a certain' (here 'a certain one'); کاخ is the direct object 'palace'; کرد is the past 3rd-singular 'did/made.' اندران ('among them' or 'among those') modifies the place or group where this palace belongs; شهریار is the beneficiary 'for the king' (or identifies whose palace it was). Word order: 'A certain palace he made among them for the king.'
segments.s654-g316: بدو is the dative/clitic 'to him / for him' (referring back to the sovereign). اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within'; ایوان is the architectural noun 'iwan / portico'; گوهرنگار is an adjectival compound 'gem‑adorned / jewel‑inlaid.' Together: 'for him, in a jewel‑inlaid iwan.'
segments.s654-g317: همه is the quantifier 'all'; شوشه طاقها is a genitive construction 'the shusheh (ornamental clusters/pendants) of the arches.' سیم و زر ('silver and gold') is a nominal phrase standing as the predicate with the copula omitted (common in classical Persian), so the sense is 'were of silver and gold.'
segments.s654-g318: بزر here is an adjective 'great' modifying the following locative اندرون 'inside / inner quarters' — effectively 'the great interior.' چند گونه گهر is a plural quantified noun phrase 'several/kinds of gems.' The verb 'to be' is again omitted, so the segment means 'within the great interior were several kinds of gems.'
segments.s654-g319: یکی گنبد begins with the indefinite 'one / a certain' + noun 'dome.' از introduces material 'of/from,' so از آبنوس وز عاج literally lists materials: 'of ebony and of ivory.' وز here is a fused poetic linking (and + of) joining the list of materials; the whole clause says 'one dome was of ebony and ivory.'
segments.s654-g320: به پیکر is a locution 'in form / in shape' (به + پیکر meaning 'in the form of'); ز introduces the source/material list 'from / of' and then three material names are given (پیلسته, شیز, ساج). The clause is a verbless descriptive phrase meaning 'shaped/made from pileste, shiz, and saj.' Note that several of these material names are rare/poetic and may refer to specific woods or finishes.
segments.s654-g281: This is the protasis (conditional clause) introduced by اگر (if). دادگر and سرفراز are predicative adjectives describing the implied you; باشی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of بودن used in an if-clause ('if you are'). The conjunction و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s654-g282: This is the apodosis following the conditional: نمانی is a 2nd-person form of ماندن (here 'remain/not perish' depending on context) and بماند is a 3rd-person subjunctive/jussive of ماندن ('may/remain'); و connects two results. نامت contains the 2nd-person enclitic -ت ('your') and functions as the subject of بماند; دراز modifies نامت as 'long'.
segments.s654-g283: تن خویش را is a possessive noun phrase: تن 'body' + خوی + ش, where ش is the 3rd-person possessive enclitic ('his/its') — together 'his own body'; را is the definite object marker marking the forthcoming object. شاه بیدادگر 'tyrant king' follows as the agent/noun phrase (subject) describing who performs the action.
segments.s654-g284: جز از introduces an exception/limitation 'except/from'; گور و نفرین are the coordinated nouns 'grave and curse'. نیارد is an archaic/poetic verb form (here meaning 'brings' or 'causes to come') and به سر ('to the head') is an idiom meaning 'upon (someone)'; together: 'brings upon (his) head nothing but the grave and curse.' Note: the lemma/senses listed for نیارد do not match its verbal use here.
segments.s654-g285: Another conditional with اگر. پیشه 'trade/practice' + دارد 'has' forms the predicate 'has a practice'; دلت is دل + -ت (your heart) and راستی is the noun 'honesty/uprightness' acting as the object/complement: 'if the practice of your heart is honesty.' The verb دارد is a present possession/auxiliary here.
segments.s654-g286: چنان دان is an imperative or hortatory use of دان (here 'consider/know it thus'); که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. گیتی is the noun 'world' and بیاراستی is a poetic/verbal form meaning 'you have adorned' (the provided senses list doesn't capture this verbal sense), so the clause says 'consider that you have thus adorned the world.'
segments.s654-g287: چو خواهی (چو = 'when'; خواهی = 2nd-person present 'you wish') introduces a temporal clause 'when you wish'. ستایش is 'praise'; پس is 'after' and ازمرگ 'from/of death' together mean 'after your death' with تو the 2nd-person pronoun. Word order places the temporal/conditional phrase before the main clause.
segments.s654-g288: خرد باید is a fixed pattern 'wisdom requires/it is wise that...' (باید = 'must/ought'); اکنون این تاج و این ترگ تو lists two contrasted possessions 'this crown and this rag of yours' (این ... تو = 'this ... of you'). The clause says that wisdom will demand both your crown and your rag when you seek posthumous praise.
segments.s654-g25: شهنشاه (subject) precedes the subordinate temporal clause introduced by چون, which here functions as 'when/as'. آراستی is an archaic/past verbal form (cf. آراست) meaning 'adorned/arranged' and governs بزم as its object ('the feast/banquet'). Together: 'When the king adorned the feast...'.
segments.s654-g26: وگر is the conditional 'and if/but if'. برسم here appears as a fused token but likely corresponds to برِ رسم ('according to/for the rite'); the provided verb senses do not match that noun reading (see senses mapping). موبدی is a noun 'mobad' (Zoroastrian priest) and خواستی is a past/desiderative 'you wished/desired' (classical past form). The clause therefore reads 'and if you desired the mobad's rite'.
segments.s654-g27: نخوردی is a negative past verbal form meaning 'did not eat' (classical/poetic past). جز introduces the exception 'except'. ازدست is the prepositional phrase 'from the hand of/by', modifying the following proper name مهبود. چیز is the general object 'thing' — together: 'he ate nothing except a thing from Mahbud's hand' (i.e. 'nothing except what came from Mahbud').
segments.s654-g28: هم is 'also/likewise'. ایمن here is adjectival/noun-like 'safe, secure'. بدی is the noun 'harm/evil' (the thing from which one is kept safe). زان = 'from that' (pointing back), followed by دو فرزند 'the two children', and نیز 'also/too' as a reinforcing particle. Syntactically: 'You also kept those two children safe from that harm.'
segments.s654-g29: خورش خانه is a genitive-like compound 'the household's stew/food' (خورش modified by خانه). در خان او is the locative/prepositional phrase 'in his khan/house (of the khan)'. داشتی is the past form 'he had/kept' (classical/epic past). Word order places the possessed noun first, then the locative, then the verb, which is typical in older poetic Persian.
segments.s654-g30: تن خویش is 'his body/his person' where خویش is built from خوی + ش (possessive clitic) to mean 'own/his'. مهمان او is 'his guest' (مهمان = guest; او = he/him). داشتی again is the past 'he had/kept' and here means 'he remained/was kept as (a) guest'. The clause thus states that he himself was the khan's guest.
segments.s654-g31: دو فرزند is the simple numeral + noun 'two children'. آن is a demonstrative/article 'that/the' placed before the epithets نامور پارسا, which stack as attributes 'renowned, pious'. Word order is typical: numeral + head noun + determiner + adjectives.
segments.s654-g32: خورش is the direct object 'stew/food'. ساختندی is an archaic past plural 'they made' (suffix -ندی marks 3rd-person plural in epic diction). بر here functions as 'for/to' (direction/benefactive) and پادشا is the beneficiary 'the king'. So: 'they made the stew for the king.'
segments.s654-g105: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. خوان here means 'the feast / the banquet' (the table/meal). اندرآمد is the past verb 'entered / came in' (poetic older compound form). به + noun gives a directional/locative phrase 'into the ...', so به ایوان شاه = 'into the king's hall' (ایوان 'hall/porch', شاه 'king' as possessor).
segments.s654-g106: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him / at him' pointing to the person being looked at. کرد is the past of 'to make/do' used with نگاه ('a look/glance') to form 'cast/looked' (کرد + نگاه = 'made a look'). زروان حاجب is the agent in apposition (proper name + title): 'Zurvān, the chamberlain'. Word order is somewhat compressed: 'to him made Zurvān, the chamberlain, a look' → 'Zurvān the chamberlain looked at him.'
segments.s654-g107: چنین 'thus/so' is an adverb introducing what is said. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here functioning as the speech verb). خندان is a predicative/participial adjective 'smiling' placed after the verb (a common poetic order). به هر دو جوان is the indirect object 'to both young men' (به + NP).
segments.s654-g108: که‌ای is an archaic/poetic addressal 'که ای' (roughly 'O you who...' / 'you who...'); it marks the relative/vocative clause. ایمن از شاه = 'safe from the king' (ایمن adjective governed by the preposition از). نوشین‌روان is an epithet 'sweet‑souled / pleasant‑minded' applied to the addressee; order is adjective/epithet after the addressed noun phrase.
segments.s654-g109: یکی 'one (of you) / someone' is an indefinite imperative subject. روی 'face' is the direct object (بنمای is the imperative 'show / reveal' — بنمای = بِنمای, archaic imperative). تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that'. زین خورش is a poetic phrase meaning 'this dish / from this stew' (زین here is a poetic form related to 'this'/'from this' followed by خورش 'stew/dish').
segments.s654-g110: که begins the subordinate (purpose/result) clause 'that / so that'. باشد is a potential/subjunctive use of 'to be' ('may be' / 'let it be'), here expressing desired result. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/so'); شاه را is the marked object/beneficiary (را = definite object marker); پرورش is a noun 'nourishment / nurturing / delight' here referring to what the king will receive from the dish (object of the purpose clause).
segments.s654-g111: چه is an exclamative 'what...!'. رنگست (compound of رنگ + است) comments on appearance 'what a color/complexion he has' (used as an exclamatory remark). کاید is a noun 'craft/plot/cunning' used here as a terse exclamation ('what cunning / what artifice'). همی adds emphasis ('indeed / truly'), and بوی خوش is a nominal phrase 'a pleasant scent'. The overall effect is a string of exclamations admiring appearance, scent, and subtlety.
segments.s654-g112: یکی again is 'one (of you)'. پرنیان چادر is a noun phrase 'silken cloak/veil' (پرنیان 'silken', چادر 'veil/cloak'). از وی literally 'from him' functions idiomatically here: 'چادر از وی کشیدن' = 'to draw a veil over him'. بکش is the imperative 'pull/draw!' (بنِکش → بک3ش), giving the command to veil him.
segments.s654-g65: که here is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing a clause. او را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to the pronoun 'he' (literal order: 'him-OBJ'); بزرگی is the noun 'greatness' and به جایی ('to a place/point') is a prepositional phrase of degree. رسید is the past tense third-person singular of رسیدن and means 'attained/reached' — together the phrase says 'that greatness reached him to a place' = 'he attained greatness to such a point.'
segments.s654-g66: که again introduces the result clause 'that…'. پای زمانه is a poetic personification ('the foot/step of Time/Fortune'); نخواهد is the negative future auxiliary 'will not' and combined with the main verb کشید (here meaning 'reach/affect') yields 'will not reach/affect it.' (کشید's lexical sense of 'to reach/drag/affect' is archaic/poetic and not listed in the provided sense choices.)
segments.s654-g67: ز گیتی ('in/from the world') is a locative/partitive phrase; ندارد is the present tense negative of داشتن 'to have' meaning 'there is not/does not have'; کسی here functions as 'anyone'/'any person' and رابکس is a proper name (the person being compared). The overall sense is comparative: 'There is no one in the world like Rabaks.'
segments.s654-g68: تو گویی is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'one would say' or 'it seems (as if)'; که introduces the reported clause. نوشین is an adjective 'delightful/sweet' and روانست is the predicative present form 'is flowing/animated' (روان + است contracted to روانست in older/poetic Persian). و بس ('and that's all') limits the claim — 'and nothing more.'
segments.s654-g69: جز از دست is the prepositional phrase 'except from/by the hand of…' where از دست denotes agency ('by the hand of'). فرزند مهبود is a genitive 'the son/descendant of Mahbud.' چیز is the noun 'thing' and is the semantic topic of the following negative statement.
segments.s654-g70: نخواهد is the future negative auxiliary 'will not'; جهاندار 'sovereign' (here the ruler of the world) and نیز 'also/likewise' note that not even the sovereign will have/obtain what is meant. The fragment states that apart from the son of Mahbud's hand, neither the world nor the sovereign will possess anything. (خورشها appears in the manuscript as a form whose exact lexical identification is uncertain here; the clause functions idiomatically to mean 'the world will have nothing'.)
segments.s654-g71: شدست is an archaic/periphrastic form meaning 'became' (شد + دست in older orthography) and functions as the verb 'it became.' از نوازش 'from/through caressing' gives the cause; چنان is an intensifier 'so' and پرمنش is an adjective 'high‑minded / noble in disposition.' Altogether: 'his hand/condition by caresses became so noble…'.
segments.s654-g72: که introduces the result clause 'so … that'. هزمان in this older/poetic usage conveys a continual or simultaneous sense ('constantly'/'at all times'); ببوسد is the present/subjunctive form of بوسیدن 'to kiss' used to express the result ('that the sky kisses'); فلک is 'the heavens/sky' (the agent) and دامنش is 'his hem' (دامن + possessive -ش).
segments.s654-g73: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” داد is the past verb “gave” (here ‘gave an answer’). پاسخ is the direct object “answer/reply.” به marks the indirect object (“to/for”). زروان is a proper name (Zurvān) in apposition to the following noun, and جهود is an epithet/appositive (“the Jew(s)”) describing the addressee.
segments.s654-g74: کزین is a fused prepositional phrase “from this / from it.” داوری is a noun “judgment/verdict.” غم is the noun “sorrow/grief.” نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning “should not / must not.” فزود is the infinitive/root meaning “increase / grow” (here the thing that should not increase).
segments.s654-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” برسم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of رسیدن ‘to arrive / reach’ (“I arrive”). بخواهد is 3rd‑person singular present ‘wills/wants’; its logical subject (جهاندار شاه) follows in inverted order, so the clause reads ‘when I arrive, the sovereign king will want (it).’
segments.s654-g76: خورشها is the plural ‘dishes/stews’ (victuals). ببین is the imperative ‘look/see!’ addressed to someone. تا here introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that / to see,’ چه is the interrogative ‘what,’ آید is the present/future of آمدن ‘come / happen,’ and به راه means ‘along the road / on the way’ (به + راه = toward/on the way).
segments.s654-g77: نگر is the imperative ‘look!/behold!’ تابود is an archaic past/existential form ‘was / there was.’ هیچ is a negator ‘no/none.’ شیر here is the noun ‘lion.’ اندروی is a locative/adverbial ‘inwardly / within’ (indicating location ‘inside’).
segments.s654-g78: پذیره is a noun ‘reception / receptacle’ used with شو (imperative ‘become’) to mean ‘become a receptacle / accept.’ The initial و of وخوردنیها is the conjunction ‘and’; خوردنیها is the plural noun ‘edibles / foods.’ ببوی is the imperative ‘smell!’ (direct command).
segments.s654-g79: همان بس is a fixed phrase meaning ‘that is enough / that will do’ (همان = ‘that/just,’ بس = ‘enough’). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that’ (here ‘that I saw…’). من is the subject ‘I.’ شیر is ‘lion.’ بینم is 1st‑person present ‘I see’ (used here as ‘I saw’ in a narrational sense). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ and دور means ‘afar / at a distance.’
segments.s654-g80: The line uses correlative negation: نه … نه … (‘neither … nor …’). The first نه negates the clause headed by مهبود بینی (‘you see a moon‑like one’), where مهبود is an archaic epithet ‘moon‑like one / moon‑beauty’ and بینی is the 2nd‑person present ‘you see.’ تو is the pronoun ‘you’ serving as the subject of the verbless predicates that follow; زنده and پور are predicative terms ‘alive’ and ‘son’ respectively, with the second نه negating them (‘nor are you alive, nor a son’) — the verb ‘to be’ is omitted as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s654-g49: چنان بد که is a result clause: چنان + adjective + که = 'so ... that'. یک روز is an adverbial time 'one day'. مردی is an indefinite NP 'a certain man'. جهود is an archaic identifier placed after مردی to specify that the man was a Jew (poetic noun juxtaposition instead of modern ezāfe).
segments.s654-g50: ز + زروان marks source/agent in poetic register ('from/of Zurvān'). درم is the direct object 'a dirham'. خواست is past transitive 'asked/requested' (he requested). از بهر سود is a purposive phrase 'for the sake of profit' (از بهر = 'for', سود = 'profit'). Word order is compact/poetic but follows [source] [object] [verb] + purposive phrase.
segments.s654-g51: شد آمد is a terse narrative sequence ('it happened / he came'); شد here has a narrative sense and آمد is the motion verb. بیفزود (from افزودن with the bi- prefix) is past 'he added' (the object—presumably the coin—is omitted but understood). در پیش اوی = 'in front of him / before him' where اوی is the older third‑person clitic referring back to the man.
segments.s654-g52: برآمیخت is a past intransitive/predicative verb 'was mingled / mingled'. با introduces the thing mixed 'with'. جان is 'soul/life' and بدکیش is an attributive adjective/noun 'ill‑fated' describing that soul; اوی is the possessive/third‑person clitic 'his', so 'it mingled with the soul of his ill‑fated (self)'. The genitive relation is expressed by juxtaposition and the clitic rather than an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s654-g53: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. با + حاجب شاه = 'with / toward the king's chamberlain' (حاجب شاه is the compound 'the king's chamberlain' in epic succinct style). گستاخ شد = 'became insolent/impudent' (adjective + شد 'became'). This clause situates time/condition for the following result.
segments.s654-g54: پرستنده خسروی is an NP 'a worshipper/devotee of the king' (خسروی = royal/kingly modifying پرستنده). کاخ شد is a compact poetic predicate: literally 'became palace' but idiomatically 'came to have/obtain a palace' or 'was granted a palace' — شد is the copular/transformative verb 'became/was made'.
segments.s654-g55: ز افسون سخن = 'a word about the enchantment' where ز marks source/relational sense ('of/about/from'). سخن رفت is an idiom 'a word went' = 'a rumor/word spread' and روزی نهان is an adverbial 'one day secretly' (روزی here temporal, نهان 'hidden/secretly'). Word order is compact and typical of epic diction.
segments.s654-g56: ز درگاه = 'from the court/threshold' (ز = from). وز here is an archaic fused particle (often read as و + ز = 'and from / to/...' in epic usage) linking to شهریار جهان; in context it yields 'to/and reached the king of the world' — شهریار جهان = 'the sovereign/king of the world'. The segment is elliptical and poetic: prepositional particles and conjunctions are compressed.
segments.s654-g137: بخفتند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb meaning ‘they lay down’ or (poetically) ‘they fell/lay dead’. برجای is the fused prepositional phrase بر جای ‘in/at the place of’ and here marks location. هر دو جوان (literally ‘each two young’) functions as a distributive phrase meaning ‘both young men’ with جوان as the noun 'young man' after the numeral phrase.
segments.s654-g138: بدادند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they gave’ (here idiomatically ‘they yielded/gave up’). جان is the direct object ‘life; soul’. پیش is a preposition ‘before; in the presence of’ linking the action to the addressee/recipient, and نوشین‌روان is an epithet ‘sweet‑souled’ used here as the indirect object (the one before whom they gave their lives). Word order places the object before the prepositional phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s654-g139: چو (here fused as چوشاه) begins a temporal clause ‘when’; شاه جهان is the title ‘king of the world’ and is the subject. اندرَان (= اندر + ان) means ‘among/at these (things/people)’ referring to the scene just described. بنگرید is a past/formal imperfective of نگرستن meaning ‘he looked/gazed’, so the clause reads ‘when the king of the world looked on these’.
segments.s654-g140: برآشفت is a past 3rd‑person singular compound verb ‘he became agitated/upset’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. شد is the copula/verb ‘became’. چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’. گل شنبلید is the simile’s noun phrase ‘the flower of (the) fenugreek’—a poetic image; the whole says he ‘became like a fenugreek flower’ (i.e. visibly agitated/pale/withered).
segments.s654-g141: بفرمود is a polite/past narrative verb ‘he commanded/said’. کز is a contracted conjunction here introducing the content of the order (‘that/wherefrom’). خان مهبود is a genitival noun phrase ‘Khan (or house/tent) Mahbūd’ (a proper name/title), and خاک is the object ‘earth/dust’ (what is to be raised). The clause frames the order to do something with the earth of that place.
segments.s654-g142: برآرید is a 2nd‑person‑plural/imperative form ‘raise/bring up’ (an order). وز here is the fused sequence و + ز (and + from/of) functioning to link the next phrase; in older/poetic Persian ز often equals از ‘from’ so the phrase continues the command. کس is ‘anyone/no one’ and مدارید is an imperative/subjunctive ‘do not treat leniently / do not spare’ (negative sense is implied by the command context). باک is the noun ‘fear’; together the phrase means roughly ‘and spare no one — have no fear.’
segments.s654-g143: بر آن خاک is a locative phrase ‘upon that earth/ground’. باید is the impersonal modal ‘it is necessary / must’. بریدن is the infinitive ‘to cut/sever’ and here carries the specific sense ‘to behead’. سرش is the direct object ‘his head’ with the enclitic ش ‘his’. The construction thus states a necessity: ‘upon that earth his head must be cut off.’
segments.s654-g144: This segment consists of epithets/names in sequence. مه here is the poetic epithet ‘mah’ (literally ‘moon’) used before a name as an honorific epithet; مهبود is the name/title ‘Mahbūd’ (literally ‘moon‑like one’). مانا can be read as a proper name or epithet (‘Māna’), and the following مه خوالیگرش is another compounded epithet/name (the final lemma is obscure/poetical). The line strings these names/epithets as an appositive or as vocatives following the decree.
segments.s654-g9: برو is an imperative 'go' addressed to the addressee. چون ... شد is a temporal clause 'when it becomes' (چون here = 'when/since'); روا شد is a light passive/construction meaning 'it is fitting/permissible' or 'it becomes appropriate'. به چیزی سخن literally places 'سخن' (speech/talk) with the indirect object 'به چیزی' = 'about something' (i.e. 'to speak of something').
segments.s654-g10: تو is the explicit subject 'you'. ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'about/from' meaning 'with regard to' (i.e. 'in learning'). آموختن is the verbal noun 'learning'. هیچ modifies سستی: 'no/any slackness'. سستی is 'laxity/weakness'. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'. The whole clause = 'do not show any laxity in learning.'
segments.s654-g11: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'it is not proper/you should not'. The first که is the complementizer 'that' introducing what should not be said. گویی here functions as a verb 'you say' (present/subjunctive/optative 2nd-person form) introducing quoted content; this item in the provided lemma list is treated as a noun, so its verbal use is not reflected in the sense list. The second که begins the content clause 'that I became learned'. دانا شدم = 'I became/was made learned' (شدم = past 'became').
segments.s654-g12: به هر آرزو = 'for/to every wish' with به as the preposition 'for' or 'with respect to' and هر 'every'. بر before توانا is a prepositional/relational particle here linking the idea of capability to the preceding phrase (literally 'upon/with regard to'); توانا شدم = 'I became able/powerful' (شدم past 'became'). The clause completes the quoted boastful claim that one has become able with respect to every desire.
segments.s654-g13: چو = 'when/whenever' introducing a temporal clause. این داستان = 'this tale/story' (subject of the subordinate clause). بشنوی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/present form 'you hear/are told' used in temporal clauses. یادگیر here functions as an instruction 'learn/remember' (imperative sense), though the provided lemma senses list treats it as a noun ('learner'), so its verbal imperative use is not captured there.
segments.s654-g14: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (meaning 'by/from the words of'). گفتار = 'speech/words'. گوینده = 'speaker/narrator' (agent of the speech). دهقان پیر is a noun phrase 'the old peasant' (دهقان = farmer/peasant, پیر = old). The whole segment = 'from the words of the speaker, the old peasant.'
segments.s654-g15: بپرسیدم is first-person singular past 'I asked'. از before روزگار here carries the sense 'of/about' (i.e. 'asked about'), so it connects the verb to the topic. روزگار کهن = 'the ancient days/times' (کهن = old/ancient).
segments.s654-g16: This clause has a slightly inverted word order: ز + نومشین روان + یاد کرد + این سخن. ز here = 'of/from' or 'concerning'. نوشین روان is an epithet 'sweet-souled' or 'sweet-flowing (of soul)' (نوشین modifying روان). یاد کرد is a verbal phrase 'made remembrance/caused to be remembered' (کرد = 'did/made', with یاد 'memory/mention'); syntactically the agent/topic is این سخن 'this speech' (here the speech is what caused the memory). So the literal sense = 'Of the sweet-souled (one) this speech made mention / brought (him) to mind.'

sentences.s654-s72: Upon that ground his head must be severed; (the line then gives epithets/names) Mah‑Mahbūd, Māna, Mah‑Khavāligarš.
sentences.s654-s5: Go; when it becomes fitting to speak of anything, do not show any slacking in learning.
sentences.s654-s6: You should not say, 'I have become learned; I have become capable for every desire.'
sentences.s654-s7: When you hear this tale, learn (it) from the words of the speaker, the old peasant.
sentences.s654-s8: I asked about the ancient days; this speech brought to mind the sweet-souled one.
